plancks_law.name: Planck's law
plancks_law.description_popular: Planck's law explains why heated objects glow, from embers to stars. It tells how much light of each 'rainbow' band an ideal black body emits at a given temperature. The hotter it is, the more light there is and the bluer it gets.
plancks_law.fun_fact_popular: If our eyes could see microwaves, we would notice a faint glow across the entire sky — this is the cosmic microwave background radiation, the cooled light of the Big Bang, obeying Planck's law at a temperature of just 2.7 K.
plancks_law.description_simple: History: in the late 19th century, physicists tried to describe blackbody radiation. Classical physics gave an absurd result (the ultraviolet catastrophe). Planck in 1900 found a formula by assuming that energy is emitted in portions — quanta. Advanced analogy: imagine an oven with small resonator doors. At each temperature, the doors oscillate, emitting waves, but a quantum 'counter' only allows certain portions of energy to be emitted depending on the frequency. Planck's law precisely describes how light is distributed across colors.
plancks_law.how_it_works_simple: It manifests everywhere: in astronomy — determining the temperature of stars and the cosmic background; in everyday life — operation of incandescent lamps, thermal imagers; in spectroscopy — analyzing the composition of matter from its radiation.
plancks_law.fun_fact: Planck himself considered quantization a 'mathematical trick' necessary only to derive the formula, and for a long time did not believe in the reality of quanta. His work marked the beginning of the quantum era, but initially he only wanted to save classical thermodynamics.
plancks_law.description: Planck's radiation law describes the spectral power density of electromagnetic radiation of a black body in thermodynamic equilibrium. It was derived by Max Planck in 1900 based on the hypothesis of energy quantization of oscillators. The formula relates spectral radiance to frequency and temperature, with fundamental constants h and k. It is the first result of quantum theory, solving the problem of the ultraviolet catastrophe.
plancks_law.history: In the late 19th century, the problem of thermal radiation was investigated by Gustav Kirchhoff, Josef Stefan, Ludwig Boltzmann, Wilhelm Wien. The classical Rayleigh-Jeans theory predicted infinite energy in the ultraviolet. Max Planck, trying to reconcile theory with experiment, reported a formula on October 19, 1900, and in December gave its theoretical justification, introducing the concept of the quantum of action h. Later, the law was experimentally confirmed and became a cornerstone of quantum mechanics. The cosmic microwave background radiation, discovered in 1965, matches the Planck spectrum with high precision.
plancks_law.how_it_works: It is applied in astrophysics to measure the temperature of stars and interstellar dust, in cosmology — to analyze the spectrum of the cosmic microwave background, in pyrometry, thermal imaging, and calibration of radiation sources. It is applicable to all bodies close to black bodies over a wide temperature range. It is generalized to gray bodies through the emissivity coefficient.
key_problems: Planck's law is semi-classical: it assumes energy quantization of emitters, but the field itself is considered classical. A complete description is given by quantum electrodynamics.
In real bodies, the spectrum deviates from the Planckian one due to surface imperfections and absorption.
At extreme temperatures or densities, it is necessary to account for relativistic effects and quantum corrections.
The law describes equilibrium radiation; for non-equilibrium processes, more complex models are required.

formulas.description: Spectral radiance of a black body (Planck's law)
formulas.meaning: B_ν — spectral radiance per unit frequency (W·sr⁻¹·m⁻²·Hz⁻¹); h — Planck constant (h ≈ 6.62607015×10⁻³⁴ J·s); ν — radiation frequency (Hz); c — speed of light (c ≈ 2.99792458×10⁸ m/s); k — Boltzmann constant (k ≈ 1.380649×10⁻²³ J/K); T — thermodynamic temperature (K).

formulas.latex: B_{\lambda}(T) = \frac{2 h c^2}{\lambda^5} \frac{1}{e^{h c/(\lambda k T)} - 1}

lorentz_transformations.name: Lorentz transformations
lorentz_transformations.description_popular: Imagine a train traveling almost at the speed of light. A passenger on the train and a person on the platform measure the length of the same carriage and the time on the clocks — and get different results! The Lorentz transformations show how these measurements are related so that the speed of light remains the same for both observers. It is as if space and time compress and stretch, adjusting to the motion.
lorentz_transformations.fun_fact_popular: GPS satellites move at high speed, and their clocks tick slightly slower than those on Earth. Without taking the Lorentz transformations into account, the navigator would accumulate an error of several kilometers every day!
lorentz_transformations.description_simple: In the late 19th century, physicists faced a contradiction: Maxwell's equations predicted a constant speed of light, while classical mechanics required velocity addition. Hendrik Lorentz, trying to explain the results of the Michelson-Morley experiment, derived mathematical relationships linking coordinates and time in different inertial frames of reference. Later, Henri Poincaré gave them their modern form and name. Essentially, it is a 'recipe' for recalculating spatial and temporal intervals for observers moving relative to each other, which works at any speeds, but is especially important at speeds close to the speed of light.
lorentz_transformations.how_it_works_simple: In everyday life we do not notice this, but at speeds of tens and hundreds of kilometers per second the effects become noticeable. For example, in particle accelerators, protons are accelerated to near-light speeds, and without relativistic corrections their trajectories cannot be calculated. Even the lifetime of unstable particles (muons) in cosmic rays increases because they move at high speed.
lorentz_transformations.fun_fact: Hendrik Lorentz received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1902 for his research on magnetism and the Zeeman effect, not for these transformations. And the term 'theory of relativity' itself belongs not to Einstein, but to Poincaré.
lorentz_transformations.description: The Lorentz transformations are linear transformations of the coordinates of four-dimensional spacetime between two inertial frames of reference moving at a constant velocity v relative to each other. For motion along the x axis, they are written as t' = γ(t - vx/c²), x' = γ(x - vt), y' = y, z' = z, where γ = 1/√(1 - v²/c²). They ensure the invariance of the interval s² = c²t² - x² - y² - z² and the constancy of the speed of light in all frames. This is the mathematical foundation of special relativity.
lorentz_transformations.history: In 1892–1904, Hendrik Lorentz developed these equations, independently starting from the FitzGerald length contraction hypothesis, to explain the null result of the Michelson-Morley experiment. In 1905, Henri Poincaré was the first to give the complete mathematical form and showed that they form a group, naming them 'Lorentz transformations'. In the same year, Albert Einstein derived them from two postulates — the constancy of the speed of light and the equivalence of inertial frames.
lorentz_transformations.how_it_works: They are used in all problems of special relativity to recalculate physical quantities between frames of reference. They allow for the correct description of electrodynamics of moving media, relativistic kinematics, time dilation and length contraction effects. Limits of applicability: only for inertial frames; in the presence of gravity, they are replaced by the more general transformations of general relativity.
key_problems: The concept of simultaneity is relative: events that are simultaneous in one frame are not simultaneous in another.
The twin paradox requires accounting for acceleration during turnaround, which goes beyond the Lorentz transformations.
Compatibility with quantum mechanics at extreme scales remains an open problem.

formulas.meaning: γ — Lorentz factor (dimensionless); v — relative velocity of inertial frames (m/s); c — speed of light in vacuum, fundamental constant (c = 299,792,458 m/s)

t' — time in the moving frame (s); t — time in the stationary frame (s); x — coordinate along the direction of motion in the stationary frame (m); v — relative velocity (m/s); c — speed of light (m/s); γ — Lorentz factor
x' — coordinate in the moving frame (m); x — coordinate in the stationary frame (m); t — time in the stationary frame (s); v — relative velocity (m/s); γ — Lorentz factor

planckeinstein_relation.name: Planck–Einstein relation
planckeinstein_relation.description_popular: Light is not just a wave, but a stream of tiny energy packets — photons. The higher the frequency of light (the 'bluer' it is), the more energy each photon carries. It is like musical notes: high notes carry more energy than low notes. Red light is the 'bass', and violet is the 'violin'.
planckeinstein_relation.fun_fact_popular: One gamma-ray photon from a distant cosmic burst can carry as much energy as a flying mosquito! But ordinary light is so weak that it does not burn our eyes.
planckeinstein_relation.description_simple: In 1900, Max Planck, trying to explain the spectrum of heated bodies, suggested that energy is emitted in discrete portions — quanta. Five years later, Albert Einstein boldly stated that light itself consists of particles, later called photons, and their energy is proportional to frequency: E = hν. The constant h is a tiny 'quantum of action', and ν (nu) is the frequency of the light wave. This simple formula connected two seemingly incompatible worlds — the world of waves and the world of particles.
planckeinstein_relation.how_it_works_simple: Thanks to this relation, we understand why tanning occurs only from ultraviolet light, not from light from a lamp: ultraviolet photons are energetic enough to knock electrons out of skin molecules. Solar cells work precisely because of the photoelectric effect: photons knock out electrons, creating current. LEDs, conversely, emit photons when electrons jump between energy levels.
planckeinstein_relation.fun_fact: Planck's constant is so small (on the order of 10⁻³⁴ J·s) that if its value were larger, we would notice quantum effects every day. For example, jumping off a curb could burn you like a tanning lamp!
planckeinstein_relation.description: The Planck–Einstein equation establishes the quantum relationship between the energy of a photon E and the frequency of the corresponding electromagnetic wave ν: E = hν, where h is Planck's constant (h ≈ 6.62607015×10⁻³⁴ J·s). In angular frequencies: E = ħω, where ħ = h/(2π) ≈ 1.0545718×10⁻³⁴ J·s. The photon is considered a massless particle carrying energy and momentum, which is the basis for describing radiation as a photon gas.
planckeinstein_relation.history: Max Planck empirically found a formula for the black-body spectrum in 1900, and to justify it, he assumed that the energy of harmonic oscillators in matter is a multiple of hν. Albert Einstein in 1905, in his work on the photoelectric effect, postulated that electromagnetic radiation itself consists of quanta with energy hν, making a decisive contribution to the development of quantum theory.
planckeinstein_relation.how_it_works: Widely used in spectroscopy, astrophysics, quantum optics and elementary particle physics to determine photon energy from wavelength or frequency. Explains threshold frequencies of the photoelectric effect, laser operation and fluorescence. In the classical limit (h→0), it reproduces wave optics; at high frequencies, it describes gamma-ray bursts and hard cosmic radiation.
key_problems: Wave-particle duality still causes debate about what a photon really is.
In quantum electrodynamics, the energy of a photon can virtually differ from hν, requiring renormalizations.
There are hypotheses about the possible variability of fundamental constants, including h, on cosmological scales.

formulas.meaning: E — photon energy (J); h — Planck's constant (h = 6.62607015×10⁻³⁴ J·s); ν (nu) — frequency of electromagnetic radiation (Hz)
E — photon energy (J); ħ — reduced Planck's constant (ħ = h/(2π) ≈ 1.0545718×10⁻³⁴ J·s); ω — angular frequency, ω = 2πν (rad/s)

de_broglie_wavelength.name: de Broglie formula
de_broglie_wavelength.description_popular: Everything around us — not just light, but also matter particles, such as electrons — behaves like waves! Every moving particle has its own wavelength, calculated using the de Broglie formula. For large objects, this wavelength is vanishingly small, so we don't see a ball bending around corners. But for tiny particles, it is large enough to exhibit wave properties.
de_broglie_wavelength.fun_fact_popular: If you throw a baseball, its de Broglie wavelength would be billions of times smaller than an atomic nucleus — no diffraction would be noticeable. But for a slowly moving electron, it can be the size of a molecule!
de_broglie_wavelength.description_simple: In 1924, the French prince-physicist Louis de Broglie, in his doctoral dissertation, put forward a revolutionary idea: if waves can behave like particles (photons), then particles might also have a wave nature. He suggested that any moving particle is associated with a wave of length λ = h/p, where p is momentum. This hypothesis was brilliantly confirmed in 1927 when electrons demonstrated diffraction and interference — typical wave phenomena.
de_broglie_wavelength.how_it_works_simple: Electron microscopes use beams of electrons with a very short de Broglie wavelength, allowing them to see details thousands of times smaller than in an optical microscope. Neutron diffraction helps study crystal structures. Even atomic orbitals in quantum chemistry are standing matter waves around the nucleus!
de_broglie_wavelength.fun_fact: At de Broglie's thesis defense, one committee member asked if there was any experimental evidence for the idea; the answer was that not yet, but Einstein himself said: 'He has lifted a corner of the great veil.' And so wave mechanics was born.
de_broglie_wavelength.description: The de Broglie hypothesis postulates that any particle with momentum p has a wavelength λ given by λ = h/p, where h is Planck's constant. For a non-relativistic particle of mass m moving at speed v, λ = h/(mv). This idea led to the creation of wave quantum mechanics and the Schrödinger equation, where the wave function describes 'probability waves'.
de_broglie_wavelength.history: Louis de Broglie in 1924 suggested the universality of wave-particle duality, extrapolating the results of Planck and Einstein for light to all particles. In 1927, Clinton Davisson and Lester Germer experimentally discovered diffraction of slow electrons on nickel, and George Thomson in thin metal films. These experiments confirmed the hypothesis, and Erwin Schrödinger soon developed the mathematical apparatus to describe de Broglie waves.
de_broglie_wavelength.how_it_works: The de Broglie formula is used to calculate diffraction gratings in electron and neutron optics, estimate quantum effects (tunneling, interference) during microparticle motion, and in scattering theories. In the macroscopic limit (m large), λ becomes negligibly small, consistent with classical mechanics. For ultra-relativistic particles, a relativistic expression for momentum is required.
key_problems: The exact physical meaning of the de Broglie wave remains a subject of debate: is it a probability wave or a material wave?
The original formula is not invariant under Lorentz transformations and requires a covariant formulation.
How to describe the 'wave' for an extended body consisting of many particles without losing coherence?

formulas.meaning: λ — de Broglie wavelength (m); h — Planck's constant (h = 6.62607015×10⁻³⁴ J·s); p — particle momentum (kg·m/s)
λ — de Broglie wavelength (m); h — Planck's constant (6.62607015×10⁻³⁴ J·s); m — particle mass (kg); v — particle speed (m/s), p = mv

wiens_displacement_law.name: Wien's displacement law
wiens_displacement_law.description_popular: Imagine you are heating a piece of metal. At first it’s dark, then red, then yellow, and even blue. Wien's displacement law explains how the color of the glow depends on temperature: the hotter the body, the shorter (bluer) the waves it emits. It is as if you move a color slider on a screen: more heat — go to blue, less — to red. The formula is simple: λ_max × T = constant, where λ_max is the wavelength at which most light is emitted, and T is the temperature in Kelvin.
wiens_displacement_law.fun_fact_popular: Thanks to this law, astronomers determine the temperature of stars by their color! Bluish stars are hotter than red ones — like a gas burner flame: blue flame is hotter than yellow. The most famous star, the Sun, with a surface temperature of about 5800 K, shines in the yellow-green range, which gives us the maximum light precisely in the visible part of the spectrum — not by chance, but according to Wien's law.
wiens_displacement_law.description_simple: Discovered in 1893 by Wilhelm Wien, the displacement law describes the peak radiation of a black body. Historically, it was an important step toward understanding the quantum nature of light. Analogy: imagine a large orchestra where each musician plays a note (wavelength). The 'hotter' (faster) the conductor sets the tempo, the higher the notes on average. Wien found that all 'notes' do not sound equally loud — there is a loudest note, and its pitch is inversely proportional to the tempo. Similarly, a body does not radiate all wavelengths equally: there is a wavelength at which intensity peaks, and it shifts inversely with temperature.
wiens_displacement_law.how_it_works_simple: The law is used in astronomy to determine the temperature of distant stars: the spectrum is measured, the maximum is found, and the temperature is calculated using the formula. It is also used in thermal imagers and pyrometry. For example, a thermal imager non-contact measures body temperature by infrared radiation; the peak radiation of a human (about 36°C) lies in the long-wave IR range (about 9-10 µm), and the device captures this.
wiens_displacement_law.fun_fact: Wilhelm Wien himself received the Nobel Prize in 1911 for his law, which became a key to new physics. Interestingly, the exact shape of the radiation spectrum (Planck's law) was found later, and from it Wien's law is derived as a special case for the maximum. But before Planck, Wien's formula approximated the experiment, although it did not work in the long-wavelength region.
wiens_displacement_law.description: Wien's displacement law is a physical law establishing the dependence of the wavelength at which the spectral energy density of a black body's radiation reaches a maximum on temperature. Strictly: λ_max = b / T, where b ≈ 2.898×10⁻³ m·K. The law was derived by Wilhelm Wien in 1893 as a consequence of thermodynamics and electromagnetism, before the creation of quantum theory. It later became important evidence for the need for a quantum hypothesis, since classical physics could not explain why the peak shifts toward shorter wavelengths with increasing temperature.
wiens_displacement_law.history: At the end of the 19th century, the radiation spectrum of heated bodies was actively studied experimentally. Wilhelm Wien, relying on ideas of adiabatic compression of radiation in a cavity, showed that the product of temperature and the wavelength of the maximum should be constant. He came to this conclusion in 1893, and in 1896 he proposed an empirical formula for the energy distribution in the spectrum. These works stimulated Max Planck to discover quantization, and in 1911 Wien was awarded the Nobel Prize. The law played a key role in the formation of quantum physics and later found application in astrophysics: Edwin Hubble used it to study stars and galaxies.
wiens_displacement_law.how_it_works: Wien's displacement law is applicable to black-body radiation and holds well for many astronomical objects (stars, star clusters). It allows estimating a star's temperature from the color of its radiation with good accuracy if interstellar absorption is small. It is also used in optical pyrometry for non-contact measurement of high temperatures in metallurgy and glassmaking. Boundary conditions: the law is accurate only for black bodies or gray bodies with non-selective radiation; in real objects, especially gases, line radiation distorts the spectrum, and the maximum may shift.
key_problems: Wien's law is valid only for black bodies; in real objects, emissivity depends on wavelength, introducing errors.
The law itself describes only the position of the maximum, not the shape of the spectrum; for a complete description, Planck's formula must be used.
At extremely high temperatures (e.g., in the X-ray range), the classical notion of the radiation peak becomes complicated due to relativistic effects.

formulas.meaning: λ_max — wavelength corresponding to the maximum of spectral energy density of radiant flux; T — absolute temperature of the black body; b — Wien's constant (b ≈ 2.898×10⁻³ m·K).

stefanboltzmann_law.name: Stefan–Boltzmann law
stefanboltzmann_law.description_popular: Imagine a stove: the stronger you heat it, the more heat it radiates — that's intuitive. But the Stefan–Boltzmann law says that the radiated power increases not just a lot, but as the fourth power of temperature! That is, if you heat an iron from 300°C to 600°C (twice as hot on the absolute scale), it will radiate 16 times more heat. Analogy: it's like having a garden hose — you turn the tap slightly, and the water pressure increases dramatically. So it is with light and heat from a heated body.
stefanboltzmann_law.fun_fact_popular: Thanks to this law, we understand why the Sun, while not so large by cosmic standards, shines more than 400 times brighter than its entire surface would if it had the temperature of a human body! If the Sun cooled to 4000°C instead of the current 5500°C, we would receive half as much light — life on Earth would be different. And an incandescent light bulb shines dimly precisely because its filament only heats up to 2500°C — if it were like the Sun, it would instantly evaporate.
stefanboltzmann_law.description_simple: The Stefan–Boltzmann law was established empirically by Josef Stefan in 1879 from the analysis of experiments by Dulong and Petit, and five years later Ludwig Boltzmann gave a theoretical derivation from thermodynamics and Maxwell's electromagnetic theory, linking radiation pressure to energy density. The essence is simple: the total energy emitted by a unit area of a black body per unit time is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature. This power-law dependence works throughout the Universe — from a candle flame to giant stars.
stefanboltzmann_law.how_it_works_simple: Have you ever warmed your hands over a campfire? The hotter the fire, the stronger the heat sensation — the law in action. In science, it is used in astrophysics to determine the luminosity of stars from their temperature and radius: knowing the star's temperature (e.g., from Wien's law) and its size, you can calculate how much energy it radiates, and compare with the observed brightness. Also, pyrometers — devices that measure temperature by radiation — are based on this law, as well as the thermal design of spacecraft.
stefanboltzmann_law.fun_fact: Interestingly, the discoverer Stefan arrived at his formula by noticing a pattern in experimental data: for the same platinum ball at different temperatures, the measured heat loss added up to a fourth power — he simply adjusted the exponent and got an exact match. And Boltzmann, who loved atomism, showed that this law is a consequence of light pressure, which was a bold idea for the time.
stefanboltzmann_law.description: Stefan–Boltzmann law: the total radiant exitance (integrated flux of radiation per unit area) of a black body is j = σ T^4, where σ ≈ 5.67×10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴) is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant. The law was empirically found by J. Stefan in 1879 and theoretically justified by L. Boltzmann in 1884 based on classical thermodynamics and electrodynamics, using the relationship between radiation pressure and energy density. In modern physics, the law is derived from Planck's distribution by integrating over all frequencies, which automatically gives an expression for σ in terms of fundamental constants.
stefanboltzmann_law.history: Irish physicist John Tyndall conducted measurements of thermal radiation in the 1860s, which led Stefan to analyze the data of Dulong and Petit. Josef Stefan, an Austrian physicist of Slovenian origin, published a note in 1879 in which he claimed that the radiation from a platinum wire is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature. Ludwig Boltzmann, impressed by the insight, undertook a rigorous proof: in 1884, he considered radiation as a working substance in a Carnot cycle and derived the law from the second law of thermodynamics, using the light pressure discovered by James Clerk Maxwell. Later, in 1900, Max Planck, in deriving his famous formula, confirmed and generalized this law, showing that σ can be expressed through Planck's constant, the speed of light, and Boltzmann's constant.
stefanboltzmann_law.how_it_works: The Stefan–Boltzmann law is widely applied in astrophysics to determine the luminosities of stars, as well as to calculate the energy balance of planets, including Earth: knowing the solar constant and albedo, one can estimate the planet's equilibrium temperature. In engineering, the law is used in designing furnaces, steam boilers, as well as in infrared sensors and thermal imagers. Limits of applicability: the law holds strictly only for black bodies or gray bodies with a constant emissivity; for real bodies, a correction factor (emissivity) is introduced. At very high temperatures, when effects of general relativity become noticeable (e.g., near black holes), the calculation of energy release requires modifications.
key_problems: The law is derived for thermodynamically equilibrium radiation; it is not applicable in non-equilibrium conditions (e.g., laser radiation).
The fourth-power dependence means a very rapid increase in radiation with temperature: small errors in temperature measurement lead to large errors in flux calculations.
For real bodies, it is necessary to know the spectral emissivity, which itself can depend on temperature and surface condition, complicating accurate measurements.

formulas.meaning: j — radiant exitance (energy flux per unit area per unit time); σ — Stefan–Boltzmann constant (σ ≈ 5.67×10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴); T — absolute temperature.

boltzmann_distribution.name: Boltzmann distribution
boltzmann_distribution.description_popular: Imagine a crowd of people on an escalator in the subway: there are always fewer people at the top because it’s harder to get there. In the world of atoms and molecules, a similar principle applies: the greater the energy of a state, the fewer molecules “climb” to that “energy floor.” The Boltzmann distribution is a mathematical rule that says the chance of finding a particle in a state with a given energy drops exponentially as the energy increases. It’s like having a mountain, and with height, the number of tourists would decrease according to a strict exponential law, determined by the steepness of the slope (the temperature).
boltzmann_distribution.fun_fact_popular: It is precisely because of the Boltzmann distribution that water evaporates at room temperature: the fastest molecules at the surface, having energy above average, can overcome the attraction and fly away. Moreover, this law explains why it’s hard to breathe at high altitudes: the density of air (the number of molecules at a given height in the gravitational field) drops exponentially with height — exactly according to Boltzmann, where the role of energy is played by gravitational potential energy.
boltzmann_distribution.description_simple: The Boltzmann distribution is a key law of statistical physics, connecting the probability of finding a system in a particular microstate with its energy and temperature. Formulated by Ludwig Boltzmann in the 1870s as the basis for describing equilibrium systems of many particles. It states: P_i ∝ exp(−E_i / (k_B T)), where E_i is the energy of the state, k_B is Boltzmann's constant, T is temperature. This is analogous to the 'minimum free energy' principle: nature prefers states with lower energy, but thermal motion scatters particles across all available levels, creating an exponential tail of high-energy states. Essentially, temperature sets the 'energy scale', above which the probability drops sharply.
boltzmann_distribution.how_it_works_simple: Imagine the Earth's atmosphere: air molecules are distributed by height according to Boltzmann in the gravitational field. At sea level there are more of them, at the summit of Everest — three times fewer, and at the orbit of the ISS — almost none. In electronics, charge carriers in semiconductors are distributed by energy according to this law: when you heat a diode, the number of electrons with enough energy to overcome the barrier increases, and the current grows. Also, lasers work on the basis of this law: you need to create a population inversion, i.e., a situation opposite to the Boltzmann one, to make atoms emit light synchronously.
boltzmann_distribution.fun_fact: The great physicist Ludwig Boltzmann so fiercely defended the atomic nature of matter that he engraved his formula S = k log W on his tombstone. Interestingly, his distribution is sometimes called the 'law of atmospheric pressure', because it directly explains the change in pressure with altitude. And by changing the temperature, you can 'pump' particles across energy levels — this is the basis of the Peltier effect and thermoelectric refrigerators.
boltzmann_distribution.description: The Boltzmann distribution is a fundamental law of classical and quantum statistical mechanics, describing the probability of finding an equilibrium system in a state with energy E_i: P_i = (1/Z) e^{-E_i/(k_B T)}, where Z = ∑_j e^{-E_j/(k_B T)} is the partition function (normalization factor), k_B ≈ 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K is Boltzmann's constant, T is absolute temperature. This law was first given by Ludwig Boltzmann in 1868–1871 in his works on the kinetic theory of gases as a generalization of Maxwell's velocity distribution. Later it became a pillar of statistical physics, unifying microscopic and macroscopic descriptions of matter.
boltzmann_distribution.history: L. Boltzmann began his research by extending Maxwell's (James Clerk Maxwell) distribution for an ideal gas to systems with an external field. In 1871, he formulated the general principle: if a system can be in discrete states, then its equilibrium distribution is determined only by energy and temperature. In 1877, he linked entropy to the logarithm of the number of microstates (the famous Boltzmann formula), which led to a deep understanding of the statistical nature of the second law of thermodynamics. In the 20th century, quantum mechanics expanded the application of the distribution, and Erwin Schrödinger in his lectures 'What is Life?' used it to explain order and disorder in biological systems.
boltzmann_distribution.how_it_works: The Boltzmann distribution works for classical and quantum systems in thermal equilibrium, when quantum exchange effects can be neglected (i.e., for 'distinguishable' particles in the classical limit). It describes the populations of energy levels of atoms and molecules in spectroscopy, impurity distributions in semiconductors, thermal ionization, diffusion. Limits of applicability: the distribution fails when Fermi–Dirac statistics (for fermions, e.g., electrons in metals at low temperatures) or Bose–Einstein statistics (for bosons, like photons) are important, where the Pauli principle or Bose condensation must be accounted for. It is also valid only in equilibrium; in non-equilibrium systems, populations may be different.
key_problems: The Boltzmann distribution assumes a classical (accessible) character of states; for indistinguishable particles at low temperatures, Fermi–Dirac or Bose–Einstein statistics must be used.
For strongly interacting systems, where energy is not a simple sum of single-particle contributions, the distribution may break down.
In non-equilibrium processes, such as plasma or biological systems, Boltzmann equilibrium may not be reached, and a more complex description (e.g., Boltzmann kinetic equation) is required.

formulas.meaning: P_i is the probability of the system being in a microstate with energy E_i; Z is the partition function; k_B is Boltzmann's constant (k_B ≈ 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K); T is the absolute temperature.
Z is the normalization factor, sum over all possible microstates j with energies E_j.

fermidirac_statistics.name: Fermi–Dirac statistics
fermidirac_statistics.description_popular: Imagine a party where each guest demands their own unique chair and cannot stand neighbors on the same seat. Fermions (electrons, protons) behave exactly like that: at most one particle can occupy a quantum state. Fermi–Dirac statistics describes how these 'individualists' distribute themselves over energies: at low temperature they fill the lowest levels first, just as guests would occupy all chairs in the front row before moving to the back rows.
fermidirac_statistics.fun_fact_popular: It is precisely because electrons obey this statistics that atoms do not collapse and the world around us has a definite structure. In white dwarfs, only the pressure of the degenerate electron gas, described by Fermi–Dirac statistics, keeps the star from collapsing into a black hole.
fermidirac_statistics.description_simple: In 1926, Enrico Fermi and Paul Dirac, building on the Pauli exclusion principle, independently derived the distribution for particles with half-integer spin. Fermions cannot occupy the same quantum state more than once — like passengers on a train where each seat is strictly individual. The distribution function shows the probability that a level with energy E is occupied, and at zero temperature it becomes a step function: all states below the Fermi energy are filled, above — empty. As temperature rises, the step 'blurs'.
fermidirac_statistics.how_it_works_simple: The behavior of electrons in metals: they fill the conduction band up to the Fermi energy. Because of this, some materials conduct electricity, others are insulators. In astrophysics, white dwarfs exist thanks to the pressure of degenerate electron gas, and neutron stars — the pressure of degenerate neutrons. In semiconductors, this statistics explains the dependence of carrier concentration on temperature and doping.
fermidirac_statistics.fun_fact: In 1927, Fermi applied his statistics to electrons in metals, laying the foundation of band theory, and Dirac showed that antisymmetric wave functions naturally lead to this distribution. Interestingly, at very high temperatures, Fermi–Dirac statistics transitions to the classical Boltzmann distribution, but with an 'avoidance effect' of each other.
fermidirac_statistics.description: Fermi–Dirac statistics determines the equilibrium distribution of identical fermions (particles with half-integer spin) over energy levels in an ideal gas at thermodynamic equilibrium. The distribution function: f(E) = 1 / (e^{(E-μ)/(kT)} + 1), where f(E) is the probability of occupation of a state with energy E, μ is the chemical potential, k is Boltzmann's constant, T is absolute temperature. The Pauli principle is expressed in that the maximum occupation of each state is one, and as T → 0 the function becomes a step function (Fermi step).
fermidirac_statistics.history: In 1925, Wolfgang Pauli formulated the exclusion principle. In 1926, Enrico Fermi and Paul Dirac independently constructed the statistics for particles obeying this principle. Fermi applied it to electrons in metals, and Dirac connected it with the antisymmetry of wave functions. Further development occurred in the works of Eugene Wigner, Felix Bloch, and others, becoming the foundation of quantum statistical physics and solid-state physics.
fermidirac_statistics.how_it_works: It is used to describe the electronic properties of metals and semiconductors (band theory), degenerate gas in white dwarfs and neutron stars (equation of state). Limits of applicability: ideal Fermi gas without interactions; for strong interactions or non-equilibrium conditions, quantum field theory or kinetic equations must be employed. It does not describe correlations beyond the statistics of independent particles.
key_problems: For strongly interacting fermions (e.g., in quark-gluon plasma), the statistics require generalizations.
It does not account for quantum entanglement, which becomes significant in ultracold systems and when modeling solids.
In disordered systems (glasses, amorphous semiconductors), deviations from the equilibrium distribution arise, requiring more complex models.

formulas.meaning: f(E) is the probability that a state with energy E is occupied by a fermion; μ is the chemical potential (the energy at which the occupation probability is 1/2, depends on temperature and concentration); k is Boltzmann's constant, k ≈ 1.380649×10⁻²³ J/K; T is absolute temperature in kelvins.

boseeinstein_statistics.name: Bose–Einstein statistics
boseeinstein_statistics.description_popular: If fermions are guests who absolutely refuse to share a seat, then bosons are the life of the party: they happily crowd into the same state. Bose–Einstein statistics describes such sociable particles (photons, helium-4 atoms). At very low temperatures, they can all 'freeze' at once into the lowest energy state — this is called a Bose–Einstein condensate, where many particles behave as one giant wave.
boseeinstein_statistics.fun_fact_popular: The Bose–Einstein condensate was predicted by Einstein in 1925, but it was produced in the laboratory only 70 years later — in 1995, scientists cooled clouds of rubidium atoms to millionths of a degree above absolute zero, and thousands of atoms merged into a single quantum state.
boseeinstein_statistics.description_simple: In 1924, the Indian physicist Satyendra Nath Bose sent Einstein his paper, where he re-derived the blackbody radiation law by treating photons as indistinguishable 'wave-particles'. Einstein translated the article, published it, and developed the idea: any particles with integer spin must obey a similar distribution, and at low temperatures they 'condense' into the zero state. The average number of bosons in a state with energy ε is given by a formula very similar to the fermionic one, but with a minus sign in the denominator, which means bosons do not avoid, but rather 'attract' each other quantum-statistically.
boseeinstein_statistics.how_it_works_simple: Photons in a box: the spectrum of the cosmic microwave background is beautifully described by the Bose distribution. Superfluid helium: at temperatures around 2 K, liquid helium-4 flows without friction due to Bose condensation. Modern experiments with ultracold atoms allow creating 'atom lasers' and studying quantum turbulence. Even in particle physics, bosonic statistics account for lasers and superconductivity (via Cooper pairs, which behave as bosons).
boseeinstein_statistics.fun_fact: Satyendra Nath Bose got the idea during a lecture: he noticed that the derivation of Planck's law was based on semi-classical assumptions and decided to derive it by treating photons as fully quantum objects. Einstein was so impressed that he not only translated the paper from English to German but immediately sent it to press, and then wrote two more papers predicting condensation.
boseeinstein_statistics.description: Bose–Einstein statistics describes the equilibrium distribution of identical bosons (particles with integer spin) over energy levels. The average number of particles in state i: ⟨n_i⟩ = 1 / (e^{(ε_i-μ)/(kT)} - 1), where ε_i is the energy of the state, μ is the chemical potential (for photons and phonons μ = 0), k is Boltzmann's constant, T is temperature. When (ε_i-μ) → 0, the distribution diverges, leading to macroscopic occupation of the ground state — Bose–Einstein condensation.
boseeinstein_statistics.history: In 1924, Satyendra Nath Bose derived Planck's law by assuming photons are identical and indistinguishable, and introduced a new way of counting microstates. Albert Einstein generalized the approach to particles with mass and integer spin, predicting Bose condensation (1925). The condensate was first obtained experimentally in 1995 by Eric Cornell, Carl Wieman (rubidium atoms) and Wolfgang Ketterle (sodium atoms).
boseeinstein_statistics.how_it_works: It is used to describe thermal radiation (blackbody spectrum is a special case), superfluidity of helium-4, Bose–Einstein condensates of dilute gases. Limits: ideal Bose gas without interactions; accounting for interactions requires mean field (Gross–Pitaevskii equation) or more complex methods. At high temperatures, it transitions to the classical Boltzmann distribution, but with a 'bunching' effect.
key_problems: Interactions between bosons in a condensate (e.g., in helium) greatly complicate the theory and require going beyond the ideal gas.
For systems with long-range interactions or strong coupling, no exact analytical solution exists.
The statistics do not apply to fermions – one must construct a theory of Bose condensation of Cooper pairs, as in superconductivity.

formulas.meaning: ⟨n_i⟩ is the average number of bosons in a state with energy ε_i; μ is the chemical potential (for systems with variable particle number, for photons and phonons μ = 0); k is Boltzmann's constant, k ≈ 1.380649×10⁻²³ J/K; T is absolute temperature.

spectral_theorem.name: Spectral theorem
spectral_theorem.description_popular: The spectral theorem is a mathematical way of 'breaking down' a complex object into simple, independent parts. Imagine you have an incomprehensible noise, and you decompose it into a sum of pure sounds of different volumes and frequencies. Similarly, any operator (a special kind of mathematical function) can be represented as a set of its eigenvalues and directions. In quantum mechanics, it is the eigenvalues of an operator that give the possible measurement results of a physical quantity — for example, the energy levels of an electron in an atom.
spectral_theorem.fun_fact_popular: The theorem got its 'spectral' name because its ideas first arose in the study of absorption and emission spectra of light: each color corresponds to its own frequency, and atoms as operators 'output' a discrete set of frequencies — exactly like the eigenvalues of a matrix.
spectral_theorem.description_simple: In the early 20th century, mathematicians (Hilbert, Riesz, von Neumann) generalized a well-known fact from linear algebra: a symmetric matrix can be diagonalized, i.e., represented as a sum of projectors onto its eigenvectors multiplied by eigenvalues. The spectral theorem asserts that the same is true for a much broader class of operators in infinite-dimensional spaces — self-adjoint operators on a Hilbert space. It's as if you could tune a piano so that each key corresponds to a strictly defined note, and when you press any combination, you hear exactly the sum of those notes, without dissonance.
spectral_theorem.how_it_works_simple: Quantum mechanics: the energy operator (Hamiltonian) via the spectral theorem gives the stationary states of an electron in an atom — Balmer series, Lyman series, etc. Any signal can be decomposed into a frequency spectrum (Fourier transform) — this is a special case of the spectral theorem for the shift operator. In data analysis, principal component analysis diagonalizes the covariance matrix, revealing the most important directions of variability.
spectral_theorem.fun_fact: John von Neumann, at just 26 years old, gave a rigorous formulation of the spectral theorem for unbounded operators in 1929, forever changing the face of quantum physics. It is said that he did it almost playfully, between developing game theory and participating in the Manhattan Project. And the spectral theorem itself hides in every computer JPEG compression, since the Fourier transform is its direct consequence!
spectral_theorem.description: The spectral theorem states that every self-adjoint (Hermitian) operator A, acting on a separable Hilbert space H, admits a spectral decomposition: A = ∫_{σ(A)} λ dE(λ), where σ(A) is the spectrum (the set of eigenvalues and the continuous spectrum), and E(λ) is the spectral measure (a projection-valued function). In the finite-dimensional case: A = ∑_i λ_i P_i, where λ_i are eigenvalues, P_i are orthogonal projectors onto the corresponding eigenspaces.
spectral_theorem.history: The origins of the theorem lie in the works of David Hilbert on integral equations (1904–1910). Ernst Schmidt introduced the notion of eigenfunctions. John von Neumann in 1929 gave the modern axiomatic formulation for unbounded operators, grounding the mathematical apparatus of quantum mechanics. In parallel, Stone proved the theorem on unitary representations of groups (1932), closely related to spectral theory.
spectral_theorem.how_it_works: A fundamental tool of quantum mechanics: each observable (energy, momentum, spin) is represented by a self-adjoint operator, and its spectral decomposition gives the possible measurement results and probabilities. In analytic mathematics, it is used to solve partial differential equations (separation of variables), in dynamical systems theory — for stability analysis. Limits: applicable only to normal or self-adjoint operators; for non-normal operators (e.g., in hydrodynamics), one uses the Jordan form or pseudospectra.
key_problems: For non-self-adjoint operators, the spectrum can be extremely unstable to perturbations, leading to phenomena not described by the spectral theorem.
In quantum field theory, operators are often not properly defined self-adjoint operators, requiring workarounds (renormalization group).
Computing the spectral measure for specific complex systems (e.g., in chaotic billiards) is an actively developing field.

formulas.description: Spectral decomposition of a finite-dimensional self-adjoint operator
formulas.meaning: A is the self-adjoint operator (matrix); λ_i are real eigenvalues; P_i are orthogonal projectors onto eigenspaces, satisfying P_i P_j = δ_{ij} P_i and ∑_i P_i = I (identity operator).

stokes_theorem.name: Stokes' theorem
stokes_theorem.description_popular: Imagine a piece of film stretched over a wire frame. Stokes' theorem says that the fluid motion along this frame equals the sum of all the tiny swirls passing through the film. It's like stirring tea in a cup: the rotation of the spoon inside (curl) is transferred to the liquid, and around the rim of the cup you feel the same motion. This is the connection between what happens on the surface and what's going on at its boundary.
stokes_theorem.fun_fact_popular: If you've ever stirred tea and seen how the tea leaves run in a circle, you've observed Stokes' theorem in action! The harder you stir inside, the faster the motion at the cup walls.
stokes_theorem.description_simple: Stokes' theorem is a bridge between surface integrals and contour integrals. In its classical vector form, it states that the circulation of a vector field along a closed contour equals the flux of its curl through any surface bounded by that contour. It is an elegant generalization of Green's and Gauss's theorems, underlying all modern field physics. Analogy: if you walk along a fence around a garden and measure the wind, the total wind force along the fence equals how many wind eddies flew over the garden.
stokes_theorem.how_it_works_simple: The theorem is actively used in electrodynamics: Ampère's law and Faraday's law are written through it. In hydrodynamics, it helps calculate fluid circulation. In topology, it is used to prove important statements about vector fields (e.g., the fixed point theorem). Even a simple calculation of work done by a force along a closed path is often simplified by reducing it to an integral over the 'bottom' of the path.
stokes_theorem.fun_fact: The history of the name is a real curiosity. Lord Kelvin (William Thomson) communicated this theorem to George Stokes in 1850, who was then the chief examiner at Cambridge. Stokes liked it so much that he immediately included it in the notoriously difficult Smith's Prize. Since then, the theorem bears his name, while Kelvin's role is almost forgotten.
stokes_theorem.description: In its most general form (differential geometry), Stokes' theorem states: for an oriented smooth manifold M with boundary ∂M and a differential form ω, ∫_{∂M} ω = ∫_{M} dω. In vector analysis this means: ∮_{∂S} F·dr = ∬_{S} (∇×F)·dS, i.e., the circulation of the field along a closed contour equals the flux of the curl through the surface bounded by this contour. The theorem unifies special cases of Green's, Gauss-Ostrogradsky, and the Newton-Leibniz formula, showing the deep unity of differentiation and integration.
stokes_theorem.history: William Thomson (Lord Kelvin) formulated the key idea in a letter to George Stokes around 1850. Stokes made the theorem widely known by including it in the Smith's Prize exam in 1854. However, special cases were known earlier: George Green (1828) proved Green's formula, and Mikhail Ostrogradsky (1820s) — the divergence theorem. In the 20th century, Élie Cartan and Henri Poincaré developed the language of differential forms, where Stokes' theorem became the foundation of de Rham cohomology theory and modern topology.
stokes_theorem.how_it_works: Stokes' theorem connects the local behavior of a field (curl, divergence) with its global characteristics (circulation, flux). In practice, it allows converting volume integrals into surface integrals and vice versa, which is critically important in Maxwell's equations, hydrodynamics, thermodynamics of irreversible processes. Limits of applicability: the manifold must be orientable, and the forms sufficiently smooth; for non-smooth boundaries or spaces with singularities, generalizations within distribution theory are required.
key_problems: The need for consistent orientation: the formulation strictly depends on the choice of orientation of the surface and boundary, creating difficulties in non-orientable spaces.
Generalization to fractal boundaries: the classical theorem requires piecewise smoothness, whereas for fractal objects (e.g., Koch snowflake) integrals may diverge or require new definitions.
Quantum analogies: in quantum physics on discrete spaces or in non-Archimedean geometry, an analogue of Stokes' theorem still awaits full development.

formulas.meaning: F is the vector field; ∂S is the oriented boundary of surface S; dr is an infinitesimal curve element; ∇×F is the curl of the field; dS is the directed surface area element (normal vector times element area).
M is an oriented smooth manifold with boundary ∂M; ω is a differential form of degree k; dω is its exterior differential (form of degree k+1).

compton_effect.name: Compton effect
compton_effect.description_popular: A photon hits an electron — like a billiard ball hitting another ball. After the collision, the photon changes direction and loses energy, its wavelength increases. This shift is direct proof that light has momentum, like particles.
compton_effect.fun_fact_popular: The Compton effect convinced the scientific world that light is not only a wave but also a stream of particles; this brought Compton the Nobel Prize in 1927.
compton_effect.description_simple: In 1923, Arthur Compton discovered that X-ray radiation scattered on graphite has two components: with the original wavelength and with an increased one. The shift depended on the scattering angle and did not depend on the substance. This was explained by elastic scattering of a photon on a free electron: part of the energy is transferred to the electron, and the photon's wavelength increases according to the formula Δλ = (h/mc)(1–cos θ). It is as if a tennis ball hit a stationary basketball — the first slows down and bounces off, while the second moves slightly.
compton_effect.how_it_works_simple: The effect is used in gamma spectroscopy to measure the energy of high-energy photons from the energy of scattered electrons. In astrophysics, Compton scattering, including the inverse effect, shapes the X-ray spectra of accretion disks of black holes and pulsars.
compton_effect.fun_fact: The Compton wavelength of the electron — approximately 2.4 picometers — is the minimum scale at which a point electron ceases to be point-like: when trying to localize it with greater precision, electron-positron pairs are created.
compton_effect.description: The Compton effect is a quantum-relativistic elastic scattering of a photon on a stationary or weakly bound electron, leading to an increase in the photon's wavelength by an amount determined by the scattering angle: Δλ = λ_C(1–cos θ), where λ_C = h/(m_e c) is the Compton wavelength of the electron.
compton_effect.history: Arthur Holly Compton, working at Washington University in St. Louis, in 1922–1923 performed experiments on scattering of monochromatic X-ray radiation on targets of graphite and other light elements. He recorded a shift in the wavelength of scattered radiation, which was perfectly described by his derived formula based on the laws of conservation of energy and momentum using Einstein's photon model. The data obtained immediately convinced the physics community of the reality of light quanta, and in 1927 Compton was awarded the Nobel Prize.
compton_effect.how_it_works: The effect is used in Compton spectrometers for non-destructive testing of materials, in Compton cameras for medical imaging (single-photon emission computed tomography). In astrophysics, the inverse Compton effect (high-energy electrons transfer energy to photons) explains the X-ray emission from active galactic nuclei and the cosmic microwave background. Limits of applicability: the formula is valid for a free electron; when scattering on bound electrons in atoms, the line profile becomes complicated by Doppler broadening and binding effects.
key_problems: When scattered on bound electrons (Compton effect on atomic shells), the line profile becomes complicated due to Doppler broadening and binding.
An accurate description of multiple Compton scattering requires solving the transport equation, which is computationally complex.
At energies comparable to the electron mass, quantum electrodynamic corrections become significant, including radiative corrections to the Klein–Nishina cross section.

formulas.description: Shift in photon wavelength during Compton scattering on a free electron
formulas.meaning: Δλ is the change in photon wavelength, h is Planck's constant (6.62607015×10⁻³⁴ J·s), m_e is the electron mass (9.10938356×10⁻³¹ kg), c is the speed of light in vacuum (299792458 m/s), θ is the photon scattering angle.
λ_C is the Compton wavelength of the electron (≈ 2.426×10⁻¹² m), a fundamental constant that determines the length scale at which quantum-relativistic effects become significant for the electron.

keplers_first_law.name: Kepler's first law
keplers_first_law.description_popular: Planets do not circle the Sun in perfect circles — their path resembles a slightly flattened circle, an ellipse. The Sun does not sit at the center of the ellipse but is offset to one of two special points — the foci. In winter the Earth is slightly closer to the star, in summer — farther away. The difference in distance is small, but it is precisely what makes the planet accelerate and decelerate. The same elliptical dance is performed by satellites around planets and even exoplanets around distant stars.
keplers_first_law.fun_fact_popular: Mars gave Kepler a real quest: eight years of calculations and about forty attempts to fit its orbit to a circle failed. Having discarded the perfect circle, he obtained an ellipse — and the discrepancy with observations reduced to a couple of arcminutes. Thus the first law was born, along with the habit of not trusting old dogmas.
keplers_first_law.description_simple: In the early 17th century, Johannes Kepler, processing many years of positional data from Tycho Brahe, discovered that the orbit of Mars does not lie on a circle. After many months of calculations, he dared to replace the circle with an ellipse and placed the Sun at one of the foci. An ellipse is a curve, the sum of distances from any point of which to two foci is constant. The flatness is given by the eccentricity e: for e = 0 we get a circle, for 0 < e < 1 — an ellipse. The point closest to the Sun is perihelion, the farthest is aphelion. The first law is quantitatively expressed by the polar equation of the ellipse, where r is the distance, a is the semi-major axis, θ is the angle from the direction of perihelion. The law became the empirical foundation of celestial mechanics and was later derived by Newton from the law of universal gravitation.
keplers_first_law.how_it_works_simple: The law works wherever two bodies interact gravitationally and the mass of the central body significantly exceeds the mass of the satellite. It describes the orbits of planets, asteroids, comets around the Sun, the motion of satellites around planets, and the orbits of exoplanets in binary systems. Knowing the shape of the ellipse, astronomers calculate the distance to a celestial body at any moment in time. Engineers use the first law to calculate interplanetary trajectories: a flight to Mars or Jupiter is a transition from one elliptical orbit to another. Deviations from the ellipse due to the influence of other planets are small, but they are taken into account for precise navigation.
keplers_first_law.fun_fact: The eccentricity of Earth's orbit fluctuates with a period of about 100,000 years due to the gravitational influence of Jupiter and Saturn. These Milankovitch cycles affect ice ages. And among exoplanets, extreme ellipses occur: for example, HD 20782 b has an eccentricity reaching 0.97 — the planet sometimes 'dives' almost into the star, then flies far beyond the orbit of Mars.
keplers_first_law.description: Kepler's first law is an empirical regularity of planetary motion, published in 1609 in the work 'Astronomia Nova'. Strict formulation: each planet revolves around the Sun in an elliptical orbit, with the Sun at one of the foci. Mathematically, the orbit is given by the equation of an ellipse in polar coordinates: r = a(1−e²)/(1 + e cos θ), where the semi-major axis a determines the size, eccentricity e — the elongation, and the angle θ (true anomaly) is measured from the direction of perihelion. The ellipse is also characterized by the focal parameter p = a(1−e²). The law describes unperturbed motion in the two-body problem and follows from the conservation of energy and angular momentum in the Newtonian gravitational potential. It applies to all objects in the Solar System and to exoplanetary systems with a dominant central mass.
keplers_first_law.history: Johannes Kepler, working in Prague with the most precise observational data of his time from Tycho Brahe, focused on the mysterious behavior of Mars. After years of attempts to describe its motion with a combination of circles (epicycles), he encountered a discrepancy of 8 arcminutes — too large to dismiss as an error. Having abandoned the dogma of perfect circles, Kepler tried several oval curves and finally chose the ellipse. He placed the Sun at one of the foci, not at the center. The result was instantly confirmed by all positions of Mars. Thus in 1609 the first of three laws was born, later generalized by Newton and becoming the starting point of celestial mechanics.
keplers_first_law.how_it_works: The law is used to calculate ephemerides — positions of planets on the celestial sphere. In combination with the second law, it gives the complete kinematics of orbital motion. The orbit is determined by six elements, among which a and e are key. Modern applications include mission design (gravity assists, Hohmann transfers), analysis of light curves and radial velocities of exoplanets, and study of the dynamics of star clusters. The limit of applicability is weak gravitational fields and speeds far from the speed of light. Near black holes or in systems with strong relativistic corrections, the Kepler ellipse is replaced by a precessing ellipse of general relativity (Einstein effect).
key_problems: The law is exact only for the two-body problem; in real systems there are gravitational perturbations from other planets, the oblateness of the central body, and relativistic effects.
Within the framework of general relativity, the orbit is not closed — the perihelion slowly rotates, which is especially noticeable for Mercury.
The law does not provide a dynamic reason for ellipticity — it follows from the initial conditions of the planetary system's formation.
For bodies of comparable mass (binary stars), both objects move around a common center of mass, and the notion of 'focus' requires clarification.

formulas.description: Equation of an elliptical orbit in polar coordinates with the focus at the origin
formulas.meaning: r is the distance from the focus (Sun) to the planet at a given moment; a is the semi-major axis of the ellipse; e is the eccentricity (0 ≤ e < 1); θ is the true anomaly, the angle between the direction to perihelion and the current radius vector.

formulas.description: Relation of eccentricity to focal distance
formulas.meaning: c is the distance from the center of the ellipse to the focus; a is the semi-major axis. When e = 0, the orbit is circular; as e → 1, the ellipse becomes increasingly elongated.

rydberg_formula.name: Rydberg formula
rydberg_formula.description_popular: Hydrogen — the simplest atom — does not glow randomly, but in strictly defined colors: red, cyan, and several violet lines. The frequencies of this light obey a clear mathematical rule, similar to the difference of two simple fractions. It is like a combination lock of the atom: only certain number combinations are allowed. This regularity became the first hint that completely new laws operate inside the atom, not the usual mechanics.
rydberg_formula.fun_fact_popular: Mathematics teacher Johann Balmer, being infinitely curious, in 1885 managed to fit the four visible lines of hydrogen into one short formula, without even suspecting the existence of other series. Later it turned out that his formula is a special case of a more general relation that 'plays' the entire symphony of hydrogen, from ultraviolet to radio waves.
rydberg_formula.description_simple: In 1885, Swiss physicist Johann Jakob Balmer found a simple empirical formula for the wavelengths of the visible series of hydrogen: λ = 364.56 nm · n²/(n² − 4), where n = 3,4,5,6. In 1888, Swedish physicist Johannes Rydberg generalized it to all spectral series of hydrogen, introducing a universal constant. Rydberg formula: 1/λ = R_H (1/n₁² − 1/n₂²), where R_H ≈ 1.09678×10⁷ m⁻¹ is the Rydberg constant for hydrogen, n₁ and n₂ are integers, with n₂ > n₁. By setting n₁, we select the spectral series: n₁ = 1 — Lyman series (ultraviolet), n₁ = 2 — Balmer series (visible light), n₁ = 3 — Paschen series (infrared), and so on. The formula is brilliantly simple and at the same time predicts with high accuracy the position of hundreds of spectral lines of hydrogen, which served as a starting point for the creation of Bohr's quantum model of the atom.
rydberg_formula.how_it_works_simple: The Rydberg formula underlies all atomic spectroscopy. With its help, elements are identified in stars and the interstellar medium: the position of hydrogen lines in a galaxy's spectrum indicates its redshift and distance. In laboratory plasma, hydrogen lines are used to determine temperature and density. Even ordinary fluorescent lamps and neon signs glow due to similar electron transitions, only in more complex atoms. For hydrogen-like ions (He⁺, Li²⁺), the formula is slightly modified by replacing R_H with R_M = R_∞ / (1 + m_e/M), but the structure remains the same.
rydberg_formula.fun_fact: The Rydberg constant is one of the few constants measured with enormous precision: the modern value R_∞ = 10 973 731.568 157 m⁻¹ with a relative error of about 10⁻¹². This is possible thanks to laser cooling of atoms and frequency measurement with ultra-stable lasers. And the hydrogen maser, operating on a hyperfine transition, is used as a timekeeper.
rydberg_formula.description: The Rydberg formula is an empirical relation for the frequencies or wavelengths of spectral lines of hydrogen and hydrogen-like ions. In its most general form: 1/λ = R_M Z² (1/n₁² − 1/n₂²), where λ is the wavelength in vacuum, R_M = R_∞ / (1 + m_e/M) is the Rydberg constant for an atom with nuclear mass M, R_∞ = 1.097 373 156 8160 × 10⁷ m⁻¹ is the Rydberg constant for an infinitely heavy nucleus, Z is the charge number (for hydrogen Z = 1), n₁ and n₂ are principal quantum numbers, n₂ > n₁. The formula brilliantly describes the serial structure of the hydrogen atom spectrum but does not reveal the physical mechanism. It emerged with Bohr's model (1913) and was finally explained in quantum electrodynamics, where spectral lines are the result of electron transitions between stationary states with energies E = −R_M h c Z² / n².
rydberg_formula.history: In 1885, Johann Jakob Balmer, a mathematics teacher in Basel, derived a simple formula for the four known lines in the visible region of hydrogen (Hα, Hβ, Hγ, Hδ). He wrote it as λ = h · m²/(m² − n²), where h = 364.56 nm, n = 2, m = 3,4,5,6. Just three years later, Johannes Rydberg, working at Lund University, proposed a more convenient form in terms of wavenumbers (1/λ = R (1/n₁² − 1/n₂²)) and showed that it is applicable to alkali metals if integers are replaced by non-integer 'spectral terms'. At the beginning of the 20th century, Bohr's model provided a theoretical basis, and the fine structure of lines required relativistic corrections, which were taken into account by Arnold Sommerfeld.
rydberg_formula.how_it_works: The Rydberg formula is a working tool for spectroscopists and astrophysicists. For hydrogen, it predicts the position of lines with an accuracy of fractions of an angstrom, allowing calibration of spectrographs. It is used to calculate energy levels, determine ionization potentials, and analyze the composition of stellar atmospheres and gaseous nebulae. Using the Rydberg constant, quantum electrodynamics is tested and fundamental constants are refined. The formula is strictly valid only for isolated atoms in the absence of external fields. In the presence of an electric (Stark) or magnetic (Zeeman) field, the levels split, and the simple formula ceases to work — perturbation theory is required.
key_problems: The formula does not explain why the electron does not fall into the nucleus — this required Bohr's quantum hypothesis.
Without relativistic corrections, it does not describe the fine structure of hydrogen lines, arising from spin-orbit interaction and relativistic mass change.
Hydrogen is the only system for which the formula gives exact integer values; in multi-electron atoms, the term numbers become fractional and lose their simple meaning.
Quantum electrodynamics predicts the Lamb shift of levels, which the Rydberg formula does not account for.

formulas.description: General Rydberg formula for hydrogen-like ions
formulas.meaning: λ is the wavelength in vacuum; R_M is the Rydberg constant for an atom with nuclear mass M, R_∞ ≈ 1.0973731568160×10⁷ m⁻¹; R_M = R_∞ / (1 + m_e/M), where m_e is the electron mass; Z is the nuclear charge number; n₁ and n₂ are principal quantum numbers (n₂ > n₁).

formulas.description: Special case for the Balmer series (n₁ = 2) in Balmer's representation
formulas.meaning: λ is wavelength in nm; B = 364.56 nm is the limit wavelength of the Balmer series; n = 3,4,5,… is the quantum number of the upper level. The formula gives the lines of the visible region of hydrogen.

first_law_of_thermodynamics.name: first law of thermodynamics
first_law_of_thermodynamics.description_popular: Energy does not appear from nowhere and does not disappear without a trace — it only changes from one form to another. The first law of thermodynamics is like a bank account: the change in your savings equals the sum of deposits minus withdrawals. The same principle in physics: the change in internal energy of a system equals the heat added minus the work done.
first_law_of_thermodynamics.fun_fact_popular: The human body is a heat engine: all the energy from food eaten is spent on muscle work, heat release, and stored in fat. You never get more energy than you consumed.
first_law_of_thermodynamics.description_simple: Discovery history: in the mid-19th century, Julius Mayer and James Joule experimentally proved that heat is a form of energy, and determined the mechanical equivalent of heat. Rudolf Clausius in 1850 mathematically expressed the law of conservation of energy for thermodynamic systems, introducing the concept of internal energy. The law states: energy neither disappears nor is created, but only transforms. An analogy with a hydraulic system: internal energy is the water level in a tank, heat is the liquid poured in, work is the liquid drained. If you pour in and drain the same amount, the level remains unchanged.
first_law_of_thermodynamics.how_it_works_simple: The law governs all thermal processes: car engines, refrigerators, power plants, metabolism of living cells. For example, in a steam turbine, heat from fuel combustion is converted into mechanical work, but part inevitably goes to heating the environment.
first_law_of_thermodynamics.fun_fact: It is the first law of thermodynamics that puts an end to perpetual motion machines of the first kind: it is impossible to build a machine that performs work without energy input.
first_law_of_thermodynamics.description: The first law of thermodynamics is a fundamental law of conservation of energy for thermodynamic systems. Strict formulation: in any process occurring in an isolated system, the total energy of the system remains constant. For a closed system, the change in internal energy ΔU equals the sum of heat Q transferred to the system and work W done on the system (depending on the sign convention). Mathematically: ΔU = Q - W, where W is the work done by the system. In differential form: dU = δQ - δW, where dU is an exact differential, while δQ and δW are process functionals.
first_law_of_thermodynamics.history: The law was formed by the efforts of several scientists in the mid-19th century. Julius Robert von Mayer in 1842 first formulated the principle of conservation of energy based on observations of blood color in the tropics. James Prescott Joule in 1843-1845 experimentally measured the mechanical equivalent of heat, proving that heat is a form of energy. Hermann von Helmholtz in 1847 generalized the law to all forms of energy. Rudolf Clausius in 1850 introduced the concept of internal energy and gave the first rigorous mathematical formulation for heat engines.
first_law_of_thermodynamics.how_it_works: The law is the basis of all technical thermodynamics: calculation of engine cycles, refrigerators, chemical reactions. It is applicable to macroscopic systems in a state of thermodynamic equilibrium or close to it. Limits: for relativistic processes, the mass-energy equivalence (E=mc²) must be taken into account; for microscopic systems, quantum fluctuations are important.
key_problems: The need for complete accounting of all forms of energy, including vacuum energy and dark energy
Paradoxes related to gravitational field energy in GR
Violations at quantum gravity scales, where the concepts of temperature and work may be undefined
Applicability to strongly non-equilibrium systems, where entropy is not defined

formulas.meaning: ΔU is the change in internal energy of the system, Q is the amount of heat transferred to the system, W is the work done by the system against external forces. The minus sign corresponds to the convention that positive work expands the system and decreases its internal energy.
dU is the exact differential of internal energy (path-independent), δQ and δW are inexact differentials of heat and work, depending on the process path.

superposition_principle.name: superposition principle
superposition_principle.description_popular: A quantum particle can be in several states at once — like a coin tossed in the air, which hasn't landed yet and so is neither heads nor tails, but a mixture of them. This cocktail of possibilities collapses into a concrete result only upon measurement.
superposition_principle.fun_fact_popular: The most famous example of superposition is Schrödinger's cat: in a thought experiment, it sits in a box with a poison mechanism and is simultaneously alive and dead until you open the lid.
superposition_principle.description_simple: History: in 1924, Louis de Broglie suggested that particles have wave properties, and in 1926, Erwin Schrödinger created wave mechanics, where the state of a particle is described by a wave function. The superposition principle states that any two possible wave functions can be added with complex weights to obtain a new allowed state. Advanced analogy: imagine a musical chord — the pressed piano keys create a sound that is the sum of pure notes. Similarly, an electron in an atom is not on one orbit but is smeared over several simultaneously, forming hybrid clouds.
superposition_principle.how_it_works_simple: All of quantum chemistry (formation of molecules), the operation of quantum computers, and quantum cryptography are based on superposition. For example, the hydrogen molecule arises because the electron 'splits' itself between two protons.
superposition_principle.fun_fact: Thanks to superposition, a quantum computer can explore many solutions in parallel, but extracting the answer is not easy: upon measurement, the entire rich pattern collapses into one result.
superposition_principle.description: The principle of superposition of states is one of the central postulates of quantum mechanics. It states: if a quantum system can be in states described by wave functions ψ₁ and ψ₂, then it can also be in the state ψ = c₁ψ₁ + c₂ψ₂, where c₁ and c₂ are arbitrary complex numbers satisfying the normalization condition |c₁|²+|c₂|²=1. Upon measurement of a physical quantity, the probability of obtaining the value corresponding to state ψ₁ is |c₁|². The principle is mathematically expressed through the linearity of the Schrödinger equation and the vector representation of states in Hilbert space: |ψ⟩ = c₁|ψ₁⟩ + c₂|ψ₂⟩.
superposition_principle.history: The roots of the principle go back to the wave hypothesis of Louis de Broglie (1924), who suggested that each particle corresponds to a wave. Erwin Schrödinger in 1926 constructed the wave equation, whose solutions were expressed through a superposition of harmonic states. In the same year, Max Born proposed the probabilistic interpretation of the wave function, after which the superposition principle acquired probabilistic meaning. Paul Dirac in 1930 generalized the formalism to any quantum systems, introducing bra-ket notation and the concept of Hilbert space, where any linear combination of admissible state vectors is also admissible.
superposition_principle.how_it_works: The principle underlies all of quantum physics: it explains the interference of particles at two slits, the structure of molecules through hybridization of orbitals, the operation of quantum computers (qubits in superposition of |0⟩ and |1⟩). It also gives rise to quantum entanglement, when the state of two particles is inseparable into individual states. Limitation: superposition persists only until the act of measurement (state reduction). At macroscopic scales, decoherence quickly destroys quantum correlations, so Schrödinger's cat cannot be observed.
key_problems: Measurement problem: why and how exactly does one state get chosen from a superposition?
Decoherence as a process of irreversible loss of quantum coherence upon interaction with the environment
Is there a fundamental limit to the scale of superposition? (experiments with macromolecules)
Nonlinear modifications of quantum mechanics (e.g., spontaneous collapse models) and their verification

formulas.meaning: |ψ⟩ is the quantum state vector, |ψ_1⟩ and |ψ_2⟩ are basis states (e.g., spin up/down), c_1 and c_2 are complex probability amplitudes, normalized by the condition |c_1|^2 + |c_2|^2 = 1.

P(|ψ_i⟩) is the probability of finding the system in state |ψ_i⟩ upon measurement, c_i is the corresponding probability amplitude; the sum of probabilities of all possible outcomes equals 1 (normalization condition).

equivalence_principle.name: equivalence principle
equivalence_principle.description_popular: Being in an elevator falling in a vacuum is no different from free flight in weightlessness. You will not feel your own weight in both cases. The equivalence principle says: locally, gravity and acceleration are indistinguishable — that very 'happiest moment' in Einstein's life.
equivalence_principle.fun_fact_popular: Aircraft for training cosmonauts fly along a ballistic trajectory and allow passengers inside to experience 20–30 seconds of real weightlessness — this is a direct consequence of the equivalence principle.
equivalence_principle.description_simple: History: a brilliant insight came to Einstein in 1907: if a person is freely falling, they do not feel their own weight — as if gravity had disappeared. This thought experiment led to the equivalence principle: locally, it is impossible to distinguish a homogeneous gravitational field from acceleration. In other words, inertial and gravitational masses are always equal, so a bird in an elevator that has snapped its cable floats in weightlessness. Analogy: if you are on a space cruise liner rotating to create artificial gravity, any experiment inside the ship cannot distinguish centrifugal force from real gravity.
equivalence_principle.how_it_works_simple: The principle explains why an orbital station does not fall to Earth: it is constantly 'falling past' the planet, moving by inertia, and people inside are in free fall — this is a pure realization of equivalence. Inertial navigation systems operate on this principle, and GPS accounts for gravitational time dilation.
equivalence_principle.fun_fact: Modern experiments confirm the principle to within one part in a trillion: in the satellite experiment MICROSCOPE (2017), the equality of masses was tested to a record level of 10⁻¹⁵.
equivalence_principle.description: The equivalence principle is the main postulate of general relativity, asserting the equivalence of gravity and acceleration. In its weak form: the so-called weak equivalence principle (WEP) states that the trajectory of a freely falling test body does not depend on its internal composition and structure, which is equivalent to the equality of inertial and gravitational mass: m_i = m_g. Einstein's equivalence principle (EEP) extends it: in any local freely falling reference frame, all non-gravitational laws of physics take the form predicted by special relativity. The strong principle (SEP) adds that the same holds for gravitational laws (except gravity itself).
