equivalence_principle.history: Galileo Galilei in the early 17th century showed that all bodies fall with the same acceleration (ignoring air resistance). Isaac Newton in 1687 noted the proportionality of gravitational force to acceleration through the introduction of two types of mass, but considered it a coincidence. Albert Einstein in 1907, reflecting on a man falling from a roof, realized that free fall locally eliminates weight, and formulated the equivalence principle as a basis for extending relativity to gravity. This led him to general relativity (1915). Later, the principle was repeatedly tested: from Eötvös experiments with torsion balances to the MICROSCOPE satellite.
equivalence_principle.how_it_works: The equivalence principle explains phenomena such as gravitational redshift, the bending of starlight near the Sun, and time dilation in a gravitational field. It is essential for the operation of satellite navigation systems (GPS, GLONASS), where relativistic corrections to clock rates are taken into account. Applicability is strictly local: in sufficiently large regions of spacetime, tidal forces appear, revealing the non-uniformity of the gravitational field.
key_problems: Is the equivalence principle a separate postulate or a consequence of GR?
Is the strong principle violated in theories with varying coupling constants (dilaton models)?
Search for a 'fifth force' — a possible deviation from the universality of free fall at very small distances
Problem of reconciling the equivalence principle with quantum field theory (quantum gravity)

formulas.description: Equality of inertial and gravitational mass
formulas.meaning: m_i is the inertial mass, determining resistance to acceleration (Newton's second law), m_g is the gravitational mass, determining the force of gravitational attraction (law of universal gravitation). Experimentally, they are equal with high precision, so all bodies fall equally.

a is the acceleration of a body in the Earth's gravity field (or any gravitating body), g is the acceleration of free fall (approximately 9.8 m/s² at the Earth's surface). Acceleration does not depend on the mass and composition of the body.

braggs_law.name: Bragg's law
braggs_law.description_popular: Stair steps and stride length. If the stride matches the step height, climbing goes smoothly. Similarly, X-rays reflect off atomic layers of a crystal — the reflected waves reinforce each other when the path difference is a multiple of the wavelength. In other words, to 'detect' a maximum, two rays that have traveled different distances must arrive in phase.
braggs_law.fun_fact_popular: Bragg's law allows measuring distances between atoms with an accuracy of tenths of a nanometer — this is how the double helix of DNA was determined.
braggs_law.description_simple: In 1912, father and son William Henry and William Lawrence Bragg showed how X-rays, reflecting from crystal planes, produce interference maxima. The equation 2d sin θ = nλ became the key to X-ray structural analysis. It's like a sonar for atomic rows: knowing the angle at which the 'echo' arrives, the distance to the layer is calculated, multiplied by the order of reflection.
braggs_law.how_it_works_simple: Debye–Scherrer camera, diffractometers — everyday tools of materials science. Without Bragg's law, there would be no accurate models of proteins, viruses, and drug compounds. It is used to determine the structure of new materials, control the quality of semiconductors, and study stresses in parts.
braggs_law.fun_fact: Rosalind Franklin obtained the famous 'Photo 51' of DNA diffraction using Bragg's method; these data helped Watson and Crick.
braggs_law.description: The Bragg–Wulf condition determines the angles at which constructive interference occurs for electromagnetic waves elastically scattered on a periodic crystal lattice. Strictly: the path difference between rays reflected from adjacent parallel planes with interplanar spacing d is 2d sin θ, and a maximum is reached when it equals an integer number n of wavelengths λ.
braggs_law.history: The law was first formulated by William Lawrence Bragg in November 1912 (Cambridge) based on experiments with X-ray diffraction on zinc sulfide crystals. Independently and almost simultaneously, Russian crystallographer Georgy Wulf arrived at the same result (1913). For this work, William Henry Bragg and William Lawrence Bragg received the Nobel Prize in Physics (1915) — the only case where a father and son shared the prize.
braggs_law.how_it_works: Used in X-ray, neutron, and electron diffraction to determine the atomic structure of crystals. The method allows deciphering complex molecular architectures — from simple salts to ribosomes. Limits of applicability: work with single crystals, the radiation wavelength must be comparable to interatomic distances; with very strong lattice distortions, the law transitions to a more general dynamic theory of diffraction.
key_problems: Phase problem: a diffraction experiment records only the amplitudes of reflections, but not phases, which complicates direct structure reconstruction.
Diffraction on imperfect, defective, or nanocrystalline samples requires additional theoretical approaches.
Generalization of the law to dynamic diffraction (multiple scattering effects) is necessary for high-precision analysis.

formulas.description: Bragg–Wulf condition for diffraction of monochromatic radiation on a crystal plane with Miller indices
formulas.meaning: d is the interplanar spacing for a family of parallel atomic planes, θ is the glancing angle (angle between the incident ray and the plane), n is an integer (order of the diffraction maximum), λ is the radiation wavelength.

spinstatistics_theorem.name: spin–statistics theorem
spinstatistics_theorem.description_popular: In the quantum world, particles are divided into unsociable individualists and sociable party-goers — that's what the spin–statistics theorem states. A particle's character is rigidly tied to its spin: half-integer spin — 'don't like neighbors' (fermions, like electrons), integer spin — 'the more the merrier' (bosons, like photons).
spinstatistics_theorem.fun_fact_popular: If electrons were bosons, all atoms would collapse into uniform lumps, and the Periodic Table would disappear.
spinstatistics_theorem.description_simple: In 1940, Wolfgang Pauli rigorously proved what was known empirically: particles with integer spin obey Bose–Einstein statistics (their wave function is symmetric under exchange), while those with half-integer spin obey Fermi–Dirac statistics (antisymmetric). The deep reason lies in relativistic quantum field theory, where locality and causality dictate this connection. Imagine an apartment building: in each apartment, only one family with a grumpy head (fermion) can live, but if sociable neighbors (bosons) move in, they happily crowd into one apartment.
spinstatistics_theorem.how_it_works_simple: The theorem explains why electrons in atoms occupy different orbitals (Pauli principle), and photons can accumulate in one state (laser effect). It is also responsible for the stability of white dwarfs (degenerate electron pressure) and for superconductivity (Cooper pairs as bosons).
spinstatistics_theorem.fun_fact: In a 3D universe, the exclusion principle holds for fermions, but in two-dimensional systems, exotic 'anyon' statistics are possible — neither bosonic nor fermionic.
spinstatistics_theorem.description: The theorem states that in any relativistic quantum field theory with Lorentz invariance and microphysical causality, particles with integer spin are bosons (obey commutation relations, symmetric statistics), and those with half-integer spin are fermions (anticommutation relations, antisymmetric statistics). In other words, the spin–statistics connection is inevitable for preserving positivity of energy and the absence of signals outside the light cone.
spinstatistics_theorem.history: Wolfgang Pauli (1940) played a key role in the proof, building on earlier work by Markus Fierz (1939). Pauli showed that without accounting for the spin–statistics connection, quantum electrodynamics would give negative probabilities and causality violation. Later, the generality of the theorem was confirmed by Lüders and Zumino (1958) within the framework of axiomatic field theory.
spinstatistics_theorem.how_it_works: In practice, the theorem determines which fields can be used to describe matter (fermionic Dirac fields) and force carriers (bosonic gauge fields). It underlies all quantum statistics, from atomic structure to early universe cosmology. Limits of applicability: strictly holds in 3+1-dimensional spacetime for free particles and under the condition of Lorentz invariance.
key_problems: In systems with broken Lorentz invariance (e.g., in condensed matter), exceptions are possible, as the theorem is not applicable.
The existence of anyons in two-dimensional systems (fractional quantum Hall effect) indicates that in dimensions below 3+1, the spin–statistics connection may weaken.
It remains an open question whether the theorem can be derived without using quantum field theory, for example, from topological considerations.

formulas.description: Criterion for spin–statistics connection: the wave function of a system of identical particles changes sign upon exchange of two fermions and remains unchanged for bosons
formulas.meaning: s is the spin quantum number (eigenvalue of the spin operator in units of Planck's constant ħ ≈ 1.054571817×10⁻³⁴ J·s); for bosons s = 0, 1, 2, … , for fermions s = 1/2, 3/2, … . The +1 sign denotes symmetry of the wave function (Bose–Einstein statistics), –1 denotes antisymmetry (Fermi–Dirac statistics).

einsteins_photoelectric_equation.name: Einstein's photoelectric equation
einsteins_photoelectric_equation.description_popular: Is light a stream of bullets, or waves? The photoelectric effect declared: bullets. Electrons are ejected from metal only if the light frequency exceeds a threshold, and the energy depends on the color, not the brightness. Einstein's equation E = hf — W precisely describes how much energy the electron retains after escape.
einsteins_photoelectric_equation.fun_fact_popular: Einstein received the 1921 Nobel Prize for explaining the photoelectric effect, not for the theory of relativity, which initially seemed too bold.
einsteins_photoelectric_equation.description_simple: In 1905, Albert Einstein proposed that light consists of quanta (photons) with energy hf. When it hits a metal, the photon disappears, and its energy goes into ejecting an electron. The work function W is the 'entry ticket' for the electron. Analogy: a vending machine that accepts only coins of a certain denomination; if the coin is smaller, the product doesn't come out.
einsteins_photoelectric_equation.how_it_works_simple: Used in solar cells (photons create electron-hole pairs), in light sensors, in photomultipliers for detecting weak light signals. It also explains the red edge of the photoelectric effect — the minimum frequency at which current still flows.
einsteins_photoelectric_equation.fun_fact: Millikan spent 10 years on experiments trying to disprove Einstein's equation, but ended up confirming it with high accuracy and measured Planck's constant.
einsteins_photoelectric_equation.description: Einstein's photoelectric equation: the maximum kinetic energy of a photoemitted electron equals the energy of the absorbed photon minus the work function: E_k = hf — W. It embodies the quantum nature of light, where energy is transferred to matter in portions.
einsteins_photoelectric_equation.history: Albert Einstein, inspired by Max Planck's quantum hypothesis, introduced the concept of the photon and proposed the photoelectric equation in his March 1905 paper. Experimental confirmation was provided by Robert Millikan in 1916 after years of testing. Einstein received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921 precisely for this work.
einsteins_photoelectric_equation.how_it_works: The equation underlies photoemission spectroscopy, which allows measuring the work function of materials and surface chemical composition. Applicable for ultraviolet and visible radiation on metals; for the X-ray range relativistic corrections are required, and for semiconductors electron affinity is used instead of the work function.
key_problems: At high intensities, multi-photon processes are possible, not described by the simple equation
The equation assumes instantaneous emission, but in reality there is a time delay
Does not take into account the effect of temperature on electron distribution

formulas.meaning: E_k — maximum kinetic energy of the ejected electron (in joules or eV), h — Planck's constant (h ≈ 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s), f — frequency of the incident light, W — work function of the material (energy required for an electron to leave the surface, typically 2 to 5 eV for metals).
E — energy of the photon, h — Planck's constant, f — frequency of the radiation.

poyntings_theorem.name: Poynting's theorem
poyntings_theorem.description_popular: Sunlight carries energy across the void of space. Poynting's theorem reveals the balance: the energy of the electromagnetic field doesn't disappear, but either leaves across an imaginary boundary of a volume or heats the charges inside. Poynting presented the energy flow as a vector, always perpendicular to the electric and magnetic fields — like the direction of wind blowing leaves away.
poyntings_theorem.fun_fact_popular: If you point an antenna at a blank wall, the energy of radio waves reflects back, and the Poynting vector turns around. And in a microwave oven, this vector points straight into your food!
poyntings_theorem.description_simple: From Maxwell's equations it follows that the change in energy within a volume equals the work of the current minus the flux of the Poynting vector through the surface. John Henry Poynting in 1884 formulated this idea rigorously: ∂u/∂t + ∇·S = -J·E. The Poynting vector S = E × H indicates the instantaneous direction of energy transfer — a powerful tool for engineers determining the radiation patterns of antennas.
poyntings_theorem.how_it_works_simple: Any wireless energy transfer — from cellular communications to Wi-Fi — obeys this theorem. It also describes how light heats water in solar collectors and how electromagnetic waves push charges in laser accelerators.
poyntings_theorem.fun_fact: Static fields — for example, around a magnet lying on a table — have a non-zero Poynting vector! It kind of 'circulates' around the magnet, but energy isn't radiated because the flux is closed. This still causes philosophical debates about the reality of energy flow in statics.
poyntings_theorem.description: Poynting's theorem is the energy conservation law for the electromagnetic field: the rate of decrease of energy in a volume equals the sum of the work of the field on currents and the flux of the Poynting vector through a closed surface. In differential form: ∂u/∂t + ∇·S = -J·E, where u is the energy density of the field, S = E×H is the Poynting vector. It is a direct consequence of Maxwell's equations, allowing unambiguous localization of energy flows in space.
poyntings_theorem.history: In 1884, John Henry Poynting, building on James Clerk Maxwell's equations, derived a relation showing that the energy of the electromagnetic field is transferred not along wires, but through the surrounding space. He introduced the concept of the energy flux vector, later named after him. Oliver Heaviside independently reached similar conclusions. The theorem became a cornerstone of radiation theory and the foundation for all modern radio electronics.
poyntings_theorem.how_it_works: The theorem is applied in the design of antennas, waveguides, fiber-optic lines, and electromagnetic compatibility analysis. It holds rigorously for classical fields, and in quantum electrodynamics is generalized via energy operators. Limits of applicability: situations where radiation from accelerated charges or hysteresis of the medium are neglected.
key_problems: Paradoxes of the static Poynting vector: is there a real circular energy flow around a stationary magnet?
Quantum fluctuations: in vacuum electrodynamics the energy density is formally infinite, requiring renormalization.
Non-locality: the integral form of the theorem does not provide information about the details of absorption distribution inside a volume.

formulas.meaning: u — volume energy density of the electromagnetic field (in vacuum u = ε₀E²/2 + μ₀H²/2), S — Poynting vector (energy flux), J — electric current density, E — electric field strength, ∇· — divergence operator, ∂/∂t — partial derivative with respect to time.
S — Poynting vector, electromagnetic energy flux density (W/m²), E — electric field vector, H — magnetic field vector, × — cross product.

fermis_golden_rule.name: Fermi's golden rule
fermis_golden_rule.description_popular: A quantum particle like an electron cannot just 'slightly' jump to another level — the transition either happened or it didn't. Fermi's golden rule gives the probability of such an event per second. It's like predicting how often a ball exits a maze through many exits if it is constantly nudged by weak pushes.
fermis_golden_rule.fun_fact_popular: Thanks to this rule, we know why LEDs glow a specific color: electrons and holes meet at a pre-calculated rate.
fermis_golden_rule.description_simple: When a weak perturbation causes a quantum system to transition from an initial state to a group of nearly indistinguishable final states, the transition rate is proportional to the square of the matrix element of the perturbation and the density of final states: Γ = (2π/ħ) |⟨f|H'|i⟩|² ρ(Ef). Enrico Fermi called the formula 'golden rule' for its universality in nuclear physics, although Paul Dirac derived it earlier.
fermis_golden_rule.how_it_works_simple: The rule is used to calculate the probability of particle decay, photon emission, nuclear reaction cross sections, and transistor operation. It predicts the rate at which an excited atom emits light and why some transitions are forbidden while others are allowed.
fermis_golden_rule.fun_fact: In quantum electrodynamics, Fermi's golden rule leads to an apparent infinity for zero-frequency radiation — the infrared catastrophe. To save the theory, physicists sum an infinite number of virtual photons, and predictions match experiment again.
fermis_golden_rule.description: Fermi's golden rule is the basic formula of time-dependent perturbation theory in quantum mechanics. It calculates the probability per unit time of a transition from a discrete initial state |i⟩ to a continuous spectrum of final states |f⟩ under the action of a perturbation Hamiltonian H' (constant or harmonic). For a constant perturbation: Γ_{i→f} = (2π/ħ) |⟨f|H'|i⟩|² ρ(E_f), where ρ(E_f) is the density of states near energy E_f. First-order perturbation gives a linear growth of probability with time, and transition rates follow an exponential decay law.
fermis_golden_rule.history: Paul Dirac in 1927 derived the expression for the transition probability into the continuum, laying the foundations of time-dependent perturbation theory. Enrico Fermi in the 1940s actively used this formula for neutron reaction calculations and named it 'golden rule No. 2' because it proved indispensable. Subsequently, John von Neumann and other mathematicians refined the applicability conditions, linking the rule to the spectral function theorem.
fermis_golden_rule.how_it_works: Fermi's rule underlies the calculation of particle decay widths, nuclear reaction rates, photoelectric effect cross section, and tunneling in solids. It is adequate for weak, short-range perturbations and a sufficiently dense spectrum of final states. For strong fields or long duration, non-perturbative methods are needed.
key_problems: Long-term deviations from exponential decay at very short and very long times.
Infrared and collinear divergences in quantum field theory when summing over soft modes.
Inability to describe non-perturbative effects, e.g., multiple Coulomb scattering.

formulas.description: Fermi's golden rule for constant perturbation
formulas.meaning: Γ_{i→f} — transition probability per unit time (transition rate), ħ — reduced Planck constant (ħ ≈ 1.0546×10⁻³⁴ J·s), ⟨f|H'|i⟩ — matrix element of the perturbation operator H' between initial |i⟩ and final |f⟩ states, ρ(E_f) — density of final states at energy E_f (number of states per unit energy interval).

virial_theorem.name: virial theorem
virial_theorem.description_popular: Stars in galaxies whirl around the center like children on a carousel. The virial theorem links the speed of stars to the force of gravity: if a galaxy weighed exactly as much as what is visible, the stars would have to fly apart. Fritz Zwicky first noticed the discrepancy and proposed invisible 'dark' mass.
virial_theorem.fun_fact_popular: If you compress a gas ball by half, according to the virial theorem its temperature will rise — this is how protostars heat up until they ignite thermonuclear fusion.
virial_theorem.description_simple: For a system of particles bound by distance-dependent forces, the virial theorem establishes a simple relation between the average kinetic and potential energies: 2⟨T⟩ = -⟨U⟩ for gravitational or Coulomb potential. Derived by Rudolf Clausius in 1870 for heat, it gained astrophysical meaning after the work of Fritz Zwicky, who used it to discover a lack of visible mass in galaxy clusters.
virial_theorem.how_it_works_simple: The theorem is indispensable in astrophysics: from measured velocities of stars or gas particles one calculates the total mass of galaxies and clusters. In plasma physics it connects pressure and gravity inside stars. Even the dynamics of globular clusters and X-ray gas in clusters follow virial equilibrium.
virial_theorem.fun_fact: Clausius introduced the virial (from Latin vis — force) as a function of the form ∑ r·F. Today, in numerical simulations of the evolution of the Universe, verification of virial equilibrium serves as a test for code correctness: if after a galaxy collision it is violated, there is an error in the calculations.
virial_theorem.description: The virial theorem is a fundamental relation of statistical mechanics and classical dynamics, linking the time-averaged kinetic energy of a system of particles to the average value of the virial of forces. In general form: 2⟨T⟩ = ⟨∑_k F_k · r_k⟩. For systems with potential energy U ∝ r^{n+1} (where n = -2 for gravity or Coulomb's law) we get 2⟨T⟩ = -⟨U⟩. It holds for stable, bounded systems that have evolved sufficiently long.
virial_theorem.history: Rudolf Clausius introduced the concept of the 'virial' (from Latin vis — force) in 1870 and proved the theorem for a gas of molecules. In 1933, Fritz Zwicky applied it to the Coma cluster and found that galaxy velocities require 400 times more mass than visible — this was the first indication of dark matter. Subsequently, the theorem was generalized to relativistic systems and quantum mechanics.
virial_theorem.how_it_works: Widely applied in dynamics of stellar systems, interstellar medium physics, and cosmology. It allows estimating masses of gravitationally bound objects without detailed knowledge of orbit distribution. Limits of applicability: the system must be isolated or in a stationary state; non-stationary processes (mergers, accretion) require modifications.
key_problems: Sensitivity to tidal effects and external gravitational fields — for not fully isolated systems.
Need for careful accounting of projection effects when determining radial velocities in astronomy.
Relativistic generalization for strong fields (neutron stars, black holes) is still debatable.

formulas.description: General form of the virial theorem
formulas.meaning: ⟨T⟩ — time-averaged kinetic energy of the particle system, F_k — net force acting on the k-th particle, r_k — radius vector of the k-th particle, sum over all particles of the system; angle brackets denote averaging over a sufficiently long time interval.

formulas.description: Virial for systems with inverse-square potential
formulas.meaning: ⟨T⟩ — average kinetic energy, ⟨U⟩ — average potential energy of gravitational (or Coulomb) interaction; the relation holds for stable bound systems obeying the inverse-square law.

kleingordon_equation.name: Klein-Gordon equation
kleingordon_equation.description_popular: Quantum particles without spin, such as hypothetical mesons, are described by waves that must respect Einstein's speed limit. The Klein-Gordon equation is the relativistic version of wave mechanics: it dictates how 'quantum ripples on water' spread and move if they couldn't outrun light.
kleingordon_equation.fun_fact_popular: This is the first equation where quantum physics met special relativity. Schrödinger almost discovered it, but he deemed it unsuccessful because it didn't describe electron spin.
kleingordon_equation.description_simple: In 1926, Oskar Klein and Walter Gordon independently derived an equation generalizing the Schrödinger equation to relativistic speeds. Originally Schrödinger himself had obtained it but rejected it because it predicted the fine structure of hydrogen worse than experiment. Analogy: the ordinary wave equation for a string has second derivatives with respect to time and coordinate; here the symmetry of time and space demands the same order of derivatives, but a mass term is added, acting as a 'tension' at each point of the field.
kleingordon_equation.how_it_works_simple: The equation describes free spinless particles — for example, pions or the Higgs boson before spontaneous symmetry breaking. In quantum field theory, it becomes the basis for describing scalar fields, where particles are quanta of field excitation.
kleingordon_equation.fun_fact: Initially, the equation was rejected due to negative probability density. Only after the work of Pauli and Weisskopf in 1934 was it reinterpreted as a field equation, where 'negative probability' became an indication of the existence of antiparticles.
kleingordon_equation.description: The Klein-Gordon equation is a relativistic wave equation for a free particle with spin 0. In covariant form (□ + m²c²/ħ²)ψ = 0, where □ = ∂μ∂μ is the d'Alembertian. It follows from the relativistic energy-momentum relation E² = p²c² + m²c⁴ by replacing classical quantities with operators. Unlike the Schrödinger equation, it is second order in time, leading to two-valued energy and the necessity of second quantization for interpretation.
kleingordon_equation.history: Erwin Schrödinger first wrote down the relativistic wave equation in 1925 but did not publish it because it gave incorrect energy levels for the hydrogen atom due to the absence of spin. In 1926, Oskar Klein and Walter Gordon independently published this equation. In 1934, Wolfgang Pauli and Victor Weisskopf showed that within quantum field theory it consistently describes charged scalar particles, and negative energies are interpreted as antiparticles.
kleingordon_equation.how_it_works: Applied in quantum field theory to describe scalar (spin-0) fields, such as the Higgs field, charged pions, or the inflaton in cosmology. In free theory it gives the dispersion relation E = ±√(p²c² + m²c⁴). Limits: a single-particle interpretation is impossible due to negative energies and negative probability density; a transition to a multi-particle picture is required. Moreover, the equation does not account for spin and interactions, for which additional terms are introduced.
key_problems: Negative energies in the single-particle interpretation
Probability density is not positive definite
Not applicable to particles with nonzero spin (requires the Dirac equation)
Requires second quantization and reinterpretation of the wave function as a field operator

formulas.meaning: ψ — wave function (scalar field); □ — d'Alembert operator, □ = ∂_μ ∂^μ = (1/c²) ∂²/∂t² − ∇²; m — rest mass of the particle; c — speed of light in vacuum, c ≈ 3.0×10⁸ m/s; ħ — reduced Planck constant, ħ ≈ 1.0546×10⁻³⁴ J·s

formulas.description: Expanded form with explicit separation of time and space
formulas.meaning: ψ — wave function (scalar field); c — speed of light in vacuum, c ≈ 3.0×10⁸ m/s; ∂²ψ/∂t² — second partial derivative with respect to time; ∇² — Laplace operator (sum of second derivatives with respect to spatial coordinates); m — rest mass of the particle; ħ — reduced Planck constant, ħ ≈ 1.0546×10⁻³⁴ J·s

keplers_second_law.name: Kepler's second law
keplers_second_law.description_popular: A planet moves around the Sun: the closer, the faster. The line connecting the planet and the Sun sweeps out equal areas in equal time intervals. A figure skater pulling in their arms spins faster — the same principle applies here.
keplers_second_law.fun_fact_popular: If the orbit were a perfect circle, the planet's speed would remain constant. But on an ellipse, Earth is 3% faster in January than in July.
keplers_second_law.description_simple: Johannes Kepler, analyzing Tycho Brahe's long-term observations of Mars, formulated the law of areas in 1609. It turned out that the areal velocity of a planet (the rate of sweeping out area) is constant. The physical reason is the conservation of angular momentum in a central gravitational field. A stone on a string: if you pull the string towards yourself, the stone accelerates; a planet does the same when approaching the Sun.
keplers_second_law.how_it_works_simple: The law manifests for all bodies in orbit: planets, comets, satellites. A comet with an elongated orbit rushes swiftly near the Sun and barely crawls far away. This law helps find exoplanets: the planet's gravity makes the star wobble, and from the variation in radial velocity, which obeys Kepler's second law, the orbital parameters are calculated.
keplers_second_law.fun_fact: Kepler himself was looking for divine harmonies in planetary motions, but found a dry mathematical law. Isaac Newton later derived it from his mechanics, and Emmy Noether showed that the law is a consequence of space symmetry with respect to rotations.
keplers_second_law.description: Kepler's second law is an empirical law of planetary motion, established by Johannes Kepler in 1609 in his work 'Astronomia Nova'. The strict formulation: the radius vector of a planet describes equal areas in equal time intervals. In other words, the areal velocity dA/dt is constant. The meaning of the law is that during motion in a central force field, angular momentum L = m r² dθ/dt is conserved, so the quantity r² dθ/dt is constant. The law is valid for any central force, not just gravitational, and serves as the basis of celestial mechanics.
keplers_second_law.history: Johannes Kepler obtained the second law during many years of attempts to describe the orbit of Mars using Tycho Brahe's data. He abandoned circular orbits and discovered that the planet's speed changes, but the area swept out by the Sun-planet line remains constant. The result was published in 1609. Later, Isaac Newton in 'Mathematical Principles of Natural Philosophy' (1687) showed that Kepler's second law is equivalent to the conservation of angular momentum. Emmy Noether in 1918 linked this conservation to the isotropy of space.
keplers_second_law.how_it_works: The law is applied in celestial mechanics to compute the position of bodies in orbit at any moment of time, as well as in astrophysics to analyze radial velocity curves of stars with exoplanets. Limits of applicability: in strong gravitational fields, for example near neutron stars, relativistic corrections violate the Keplerian picture; in many-body systems, orbits are perturbed, and the law holds only approximately. When the potential deviates from spherical symmetry (flattened galaxies), the areal velocity is not strictly conserved.
key_problems: Relativistic effects in strong fields (perihelion shift of Mercury) require corrections to the law of areas.
In systems of three or more bodies, orbits are chaotic, and the area is swept out unevenly over long intervals.
For exoplanets, the radial velocity method gives only the velocity projection; reconstruction of the three-dimensional orbit is ambiguous.

formulas.meaning: dA/dt is the time derivative of area, r is the distance from the center of force, dθ/dt is the angular velocity, L is the angular momentum of the body, m is its mass
r is the distance, θ is the angular coordinate, \(\dot{\theta}\) = dθ/dt is the angular velocity, h is a constant equal to twice the areal velocity (h = 2 dA/dt)

bells_theorem.name: Bell's theorem
bells_theorem.description_popular: Entangled particles behave as if connected by an invisible thread. Measuring one instantly determines the state of the other, no matter how far apart they are. Bell's theorem proves that this is not a trick: no prearranged plan can explain such correlation without faster-than-light interaction.
bells_theorem.fun_fact_popular: Imagine two suspects who give identical answers during interrogation, even without conspiring. If they had prearranged, the correlation of their answers could not exceed a certain limit — Bell's inequality. Experiments show this limit is violated.
bells_theorem.description_simple: In 1964, John Stewart Bell, a physicist at CERN, posed the question: can quantum mechanics be supplemented with hidden variables to restore determinism and locality? He derived inequalities for the measurement correlations of entangled particles. Local realistic theories must satisfy them, while quantum mechanics predicts violation. Experiments by Alain Aspect and others confirmed the quantum predictions: local realism is untenable.
bells_theorem.how_it_works_simple: Bell inequalities are tested on pairs of photons. Entangled photons fly apart, and in remote laboratories their polarization is measured at different angles. Statistical analysis of correlations shows whether they exceed the classical limit. If so, it means the particles truly interact non-locally. This forms the basis of quantum cryptography — an eavesdropper cannot listen in without being detected because they would disrupt the correlations.
bells_theorem.fun_fact: Bell himself was convinced that experiment would confirm quantum mechanics. For a long time his work remained in the shadows, and serious tests began only in the 1970s. For the experimental confirmation of the violation of Bell's inequalities, Alain Aspect, John Clauser, and Anton Zeilinger received the Nobel Prize in 2022.
bells_theorem.description: Bell's theorem is a fundamental result about the impossibility of local hidden variables. John Stewart Bell in 1964 showed that any theory satisfying the principles of locality and realism imposes constraints on the measurement correlations of entangled particles. Quantum mechanics violates these constraints. For a system of two particles and measurements in directions a, a', b, b', the CHSH inequality holds: |E(a,b) — E(a,b')| + |E(a',b) + E(a',b')| ≤ 2. Quantum predictions give a value up to 2√2 ≈ 2.828. Numerous experiments, starting with the work of Alain Aspect in the 1980s, have confirmed the violation, ruling out local hidden variables.
bells_theorem.history: John Stewart Bell, a CERN employee, published in 1964 an article 'On the Einstein Podolsky Rosen paradox', in which he derived inequalities to test locality. The starting point was the Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen paradox (1935), which asserted the incompleteness of quantum mechanics. The first experimental tests were conducted by John Clauser and Stuart Freedman in 1972, but the decisive experiments, closing most loopholes, were performed by Alain Aspect (1982). Later work by Anton Zeilinger and others strengthened the conclusion. For these achievements, Aspect, Clauser, and Zeilinger were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2022.
bells_theorem.how_it_works: Bell inequalities are used to test the foundations of quantum mechanics and in quantum technologies: quantum key distribution (providing provable security), generation of truly random numbers, quantum teleportation. Limits of applicability are related to experimental loopholes: not all particles are detected (detection loophole), freedom of choice of settings may be limited, superdeterministic predetermination is possible. A completely loophole-free test remains technically challenging, but progress continues.
key_problems: Experimental loopholes: detection, freedom of choice, superdeterminism — no experiment has yet closed them simultaneously and absolutely.
The theorem does not rule out non-local hidden variables, such as the de Broglie–Bohm theory.
Violation of Bell's inequality does not prove faster-than-light information transfer; it only means the absence of local realism.

formulas.meaning: E(a,b) is the expectation value of the product of the measurement outcomes (±1) for analyzer orientations a and b; a, a', b, b' are different measurement directions for the two particles. Violation of the inequality means that the predictions of quantum mechanics cannot be reproduced by a local realistic theory.

pythagorean_theorem.name: Pythagorean theorem
pythagorean_theorem.description_popular: If you add the areas of the squares on the legs, you get the area of the square on the hypotenuse. Simple geometry: walk 3 meters east and 4 meters north — you are exactly 5 meters from the starting point.
pythagorean_theorem.fun_fact_popular: The Babylonian clay tablet Plimpton 322 (1800 BC) contains a list of Pythagorean triples — the theorem was known a thousand years before Pythagoras.
pythagorean_theorem.description_simple: The Pythagorean theorem is the foundation of geometry: in a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the legs. It is attributed to Pythagoras (6th century BC), although evidence from Babylon shows earlier familiarity. The first rigorous proof was given by Euclid in the 'Elements'. In physics, it determines the distance between points, the interval in relativity theory, and the norm of a vector in quantum mechanics.
pythagorean_theorem.how_it_works_simple: The theorem is indispensable in distance calculations: from determining the length of a ladder to computing orbital trajectories. In special relativity, the spacetime interval unites time and space with a minus sign — this is a pseudo-Pythagorean structure. In the Hilbert space of quantum mechanics, the probability of finding a particle relies on the square of the modulus of the wave function, which follows directly from the Pythagorean norm.
pythagorean_theorem.fun_fact: More than 400 proofs are known — from Euclid's geometric constructions to Einstein's elegant reasoning based on similarity of triangles. Leonardo da Vinci proposed his own proof using areas.
pythagorean_theorem.description: The Pythagorean theorem is a geometric theorem stating that in the Euclidean plane, for a right triangle with legs a, b and hypotenuse c, the relation a² + b² = c² holds. In n-dimensional Euclidean space, the squared distance ds² = dx₁² + ... + dx_n² defines the metric. In Riemannian geometry and general relativity, the metric is generalized to ds² = g_ij dx^i dx^j, where g_ij is the metric tensor describing curved spacetime. In Hilbert space, the inner product that defines probability amplitudes is based on the same quadratic structure.
pythagorean_theorem.history: The theorem has been known since ancient times. Babylonian mathematicians around 1800 BC used triples of integers satisfying the relation to construct right angles. The school of Pythagoras (6th–5th centuries BC) is credited with the first proof and giving the theorem philosophical significance. Euclid in the 'Elements' (c. 300 BC, Book I, Proposition 47) gave a classical geometric proof. In the non-Euclidean geometries of Lobachevsky and Bolyai, the theorem no longer holds, and in the 20th century it was generalized to curved manifolds and became the foundation of the metric tensor in general relativity.
pythagorean_theorem.how_it_works: The theorem is constantly applied in physics: distance calculations in mechanics, determining the interval in special relativity, constructing the metric in general relativity. In quantum mechanics, the norm of state vectors, calculated Pythagorean-way, ensures conservation of total probability. Limits of applicability: it holds strictly only in flat Euclidean space; in curved manifolds it works locally in infinitesimal neighborhoods. In Minkowski spacetime, the signs in front of squares differ, leading to null intervals for lightlike paths.
key_problems: In non-Euclidean geometries, the theorem does not hold; its role is taken by local metric relations.
In spaces with indefinite metric (as in special relativity), the sum of squares can be negative, requiring the concept of signature.
Historical priority is disputed: ancient Egyptians and Babylonians knew triples long before Pythagoras.

formulas.meaning: a, b are the lengths of the legs, c is the length of the hypotenuse of a right triangle
|v| is the length (norm) of vector v in Euclidean space, v_i are its components
ds is the interval element, g_{ij} is the metric tensor (generalization of the identity matrix in Euclidean space), dx^i, dx^j are coordinate differentials; summation over repeated indices is implied

eulers_formula.name: Euler's formula
eulers_formula.description_popular: The complex exponential is a time machine for waves. e^(iθ) = cosθ + i sinθ means that multiplying by e^(iθ) rotates a number by angle θ, combining rotation and growth.
eulers_formula.fun_fact_popular: The most beautiful equation in mathematics: e^(iπ) + 1 = 0 brings together five important numbers — e, i, π, 1, 0 — in one equality.
eulers_formula.description_simple: In 1748, Leonhard Euler in his work 'Introductio in analysin infinitorum' showed how the series for the exponential with an imaginary argument decomposes into cosine and sine. He essentially established that the complex exponential describes harmonic oscillation. An analogy: tracing a number on the complex plane, the exponential 'unfolds' it into a spiral, and with a purely imaginary exponent — into a circle.
eulers_formula.how_it_works_simple: Euler's formula underlies the analysis of AC circuits, where impedances become complex. In quantum mechanics, wave functions are written as e^(i(kx-ωt)). Any musical chord can be decomposed into pure tones thanks to Fourier series, based on Euler's formula.
eulers_formula.fun_fact: Richard Feynman called this equation 'our jewel' and considered it the pinnacle of mathematical beauty.
eulers_formula.description: The analytic continuation of the exponential function into the complex domain reveals a deep connection between algebraic operations and geometric transformations. Euler's formula e^(iθ) = cosθ + i sinθ establishes an isomorphism between complex numbers on the unit circle and rotations in ℝ², and the identity e^(iπ) + 1 = 0 demonstrates the unity of the additive and multiplicative worlds.
eulers_formula.history: Leonhard Euler in the 1740s experimented with series and discovered that substituting ix into the series of e^x yields two familiar series. In 1748, he published this result in 'Introductio in analysin infinitorum'. Before him, Roger Cotes had derived similar relations, but it was Euler who realized the full power of the complex exponential. Later, the formula became the key to creating complex analysis.
eulers_formula.how_it_works: Euler's formula is used to represent oscillations in physics and engineering: from describing electromagnetic waves to fast Fourier transform algorithms. Its limits of applicability arise when moving to non-commutative algebras, such as quaternions, where the exponential does not decompose simply into trigonometric functions.
key_problems: Generalization to matrix exponentials requires taking the commutator into account
With the complex logarithm, multivaluedness arises, critical for the theory of Riemann surfaces
In quantum mechanics with non-Hermitian operators, the formula gives non-unitary evolution

formulas.meaning: e is the base of the natural logarithm (approximately 2.71828), i is the imaginary unit (i² = −1), θ is a real number (angle in radians), cosθ and sinθ are trigonometric functions.
π is the number pi (approximately 3.14159), 0 is zero, 1 is one.

chandrasekhar_limit.name: Chandrasekhar limit
chandrasekhar_limit.description_popular: A white dwarf is a star the size of Earth, but with the mass of the Sun. It resists gravity with the help of quantum pressure from electrons. The Chandrasekhar limit is the mass at which electrons 'give up', and the star collapses into a neutron star or black hole. Roughly speaking, 1.4 solar masses.
chandrasekhar_limit.fun_fact_popular: It is because of this limit that exploding white dwarfs have become cosmic 'beacons' by which the expansion of the Universe is measured.
chandrasekhar_limit.description_simple: In 1930, the twenty-year-old Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar, on a ship from India to England, realized that the speed of electrons in a very dense white dwarf approaches the speed of light. Combining special relativity and quantum statistics, he derived the maximum mass. An analogy: it is like a spring that stiffens the harder you compress it, but breaks under too much load.
chandrasekhar_limit.how_it_works_simple: In a white dwarf, electrons fill all the lowest energy levels (Pauli principle). When compressed, they acquire enormous kinetic energy, creating pressure. If the mass exceeds the limit, even relativistic electrons cannot hold off collapse, and the core becomes a neutron star.
chandrasekhar_limit.fun_fact: Chandrasekhar on that voyage was ahead of official science, but faced fierce criticism from Arthur Eddington, who believed that nature must prevent collapse.
chandrasekhar_limit.description: The Chandrasekhar limit is the maximum mass of a star for which the pressure of degenerate electron gas, calculated using relativistic laws, can balance gravitational contraction. Expressed as M_Ch ≈ 1.4 M_☉ for carbon-oxygen white dwarfs.
chandrasekhar_limit.history: Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar in 1930, while sailing to Cambridge, solved the equations of stellar structure taking into account special relativity and Fermi-Dirac quantum statistics. He found that above a certain mass, equilibrium is impossible. His conclusions were ridiculed by Eddington, but later, in 1983, Chandrasekhar received the Nobel Prize for this and other work.
chandrasekhar_limit.how_it_works: It is applied to understand stellar evolution, classify white dwarfs, estimate the age of star clusters, and as standardization of type Ia supernovae in cosmology. Limitation: in close binary systems, accretion can cause the white dwarf to exceed the limit, triggering a thermonuclear explosion.
key_problems: The exact value of the limit depends on the chemical composition and the electron interaction model
In strong magnetic fields and rotation, the limit can change
General relativity is not taken into account in the classical derivation, which requires corrections for neutron stars

formulas.meaning: ω_3^0 is the Lane-Emden constant for a polytrope with index n=3 (approximately 2.018), ℏ is the reduced Planck constant (ℏ ≈ 1.0546×10⁻³⁴ J·s), c is the speed of light (c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s), G is the gravitational constant (G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²), μ_e is the mean molecular mass per electron (for fully ionized carbon μ_e = 2), m_H is the mass of the hydrogen atom (m_H ≈ 1.673×10⁻²⁷ kg).
M_☉ is the solar mass (M_☉ ≈ 1.989×10³⁰ kg), numerical value about 1.4 solar masses.

no_hair_theorem.name: no-hair theorem
no_hair_theorem.mini: A black hole is a perfect vacuum: any details of what fell into it disappear, and it remembers only mass, rotation, and electric charge. The no-hair theorem asserts that black holes have no other distinguishing features.
no_hair_theorem.practical_application: Astrophysicists determine the parameters of real black holes from the motion of surrounding stars and the radiation of accretion disks, confident that they are not missing anything.
no_hair_theorem.description_popular: In the 1960s, after the discovery of exact solutions of Einstein's equations for rotating black holes, physicists noticed an amazing property: no matter how complex the initial conditions of collapse, the external gravitational field stabilizes to a form determined only by mass, angular momentum, and charge. John Wheeler dubbed this the no-hair theorem—a black hole has no other 'protrusions' of information. A series of mathematical theorems proved by Israel, Carter, and Hawking confirmed this for stationary black holes.
no_hair_theorem.fun_fact_popular: The theorem gave rise to a joke: if a black hole had hair, it could violate the laws of physics by allowing one to guess what fell into it, and thus the information.
no_hair_theorem.description_simple: Black holes are like eggs from the same breed of chicken: from the outside they are all the same, we only know their size, rotation rate, and color. The 'no-hair' theorem says that after an object collapses into a black hole, all information about its former appearance—what it was made of, what shape it was—disappears. Only three characteristics remain: mass (how much matter is 'involved'), angular momentum (how much the space is twisted), and electric charge. It is as if a car turned into a sphere, retaining only its weight, the rotation of its wheels, and the voltage of its battery.
no_hair_theorem.how_it_works_simple: Observations of binary systems with a black hole reveal the orbit of the companion star, from which the mass of the invisible object is calculated. If an accretion disk exists, its shape and radiation indicate rotation. The charge is small and usually insignificant.
no_hair_theorem.fun_fact: The term 'no-hair' has become so established that physicists speak of 'bald' black holes, and any attempts to find deviations are called 'searches for hair'.
no_hair_theorem.description: The no-hair theorem states that any stationary black hole solution of general relativity, satisfying conditions of regularity and no matter outside the horizon, is completely characterized by three classical parameters: mass M, angular momentum J, and electric charge Q. This means that the entire external spacetime geometry is uniquely determined by the Kerr–Newman solution, and all other multipole moments vanish. The theorem relies on the uniqueness of Einstein–Maxwell solutions under natural physical boundary conditions.
no_hair_theorem.history: The roots of the theorem go back to 1967, when Werner Israel showed that a static, non-rotating, uncharged black hole must be spherically symmetric (the Schwarzschild solution). Then Brandon Carter and Stephen Hawking generalized the proof to include rotation and charge. John Archibald Wheeler, a master of memorable names, coined the term 'a black hole has no hair' in 1971. Subsequently, the theorem became a cornerstone for the holographic principle and discussions of the information paradox.
no_hair_theorem.how_it_works: Practically, this allows modeling astrophysical black holes with just a few parameters, which is used in estimates from gravitational-wave signals and shadows from the Event Horizon Telescope. However, the strict theorem is proven for stationary spacetimes with an electromagnetic field; inclusion of other fields or quantum effects can generate 'hair'. For example, in scalar-tensor theories of gravity, scalar hair may exist.
key_problems: In quantum gravity, black holes may have additional 'hair' storing information, which leads to a resolution of the information loss paradox.
The theorem does not cover nonstationary merger processes and the initial collapse stage.
The existence of 'soft hair' in low-energy quantum gravity is actively debated.

formulas.description: Characteristic radius of the event horizon
formulas.meaning: M is the mass of the black hole in geometric units (in the G=c=1 system); a = J/M is the specific angular momentum in the same units (J is the total angular momentum); Q is the electric charge in geometric units. To convert to SI: M → GM/c^2, a → J/(M c), Q → G^{1/2} Q / c^2, where G is the gravitational constant (6.67430×10^{-11} m³·kg⁻¹·s⁻²), c is the speed of light (299,792,458 m/s)

fermi_acceleration.name: Fermi acceleration
fermi_acceleration.mini: Cosmic rays are accelerated to enormous energies by reflecting off moving magnetic clouds, like a tennis ball between approaching rackets. Fermi acceleration explains why there are so many fast particles in the Universe.
fermi_acceleration.practical_application: Understanding the mechanism helps predict the radiation environment in space and design protection for satellite electronics.
fermi_acceleration.description_popular: Enrico Fermi in 1949 proposed an elegant explanation for the origin of galactic cosmic rays: charged particles scatter off inhomogeneities in the interstellar magnetic field, gaining energy each time they are reflected by a moving front. Initially he estimated the effect from random clouds (second-order acceleration), but later it turned out that acceleration at shock waves (first-order) is much more powerful, where a particle crosses the front back and forth, each time drawing energy. The spectrum of such particles turns out to be a power law with an index of about -2, which brilliantly matches observations.
fermi_acceleration.fun_fact_popular: The most energetic cosmic rays are accelerated to energies millions of times greater than the capabilities of the Large Hadron Collider, and it is still a mystery what cosmic accelerator can do this.
fermi_acceleration.description_simple: In space, clouds of magnetized plasma—supernova remnants, solar wind—float around. When a charged particle (e.g., a proton) enters such a cloud, it reflects off it like a wall. If the cloud is moving towards the particle, the reflection adds energy—like a ball bouncing off a moving racket. Billions of such random collisions gradually accelerate particles to near-light speeds.
fermi_acceleration.how_it_works_simple: This is how cosmic rays that bombard Earth are formed. In supernova shock waves, acceleration is especially effective because the magnetic 'mirrors' converge and reflect particles repeatedly.
fermi_acceleration.fun_fact: Fermi proposed his idea after a discussion with a colleague about the origin of cosmic rays, exclaiming: 'Eureka! Why not use acceleration from collisions with magnetic clouds?'
fermi_acceleration.description: The Fermi mechanism describes the stochastic acceleration of charged particles in astrophysical plasma due to repeated collisions with moving magnetic inhomogeneities or shock waves. In the case of acceleration at a shock front (diffusive shock acceleration), particles scatter on magnetohydrodynamic turbulence upstream and downstream, accumulating energy like gas compressed between pistons. The average relative energy gain is proportional to the shock speed, and after many cycles the energy spectrum takes a universal power-law form N(E) ∝ E^{-p} with p = (r+2)/(r-1) for compression ratio r, which gives p ≈ 2 for strong adiabatic shock waves.
fermi_acceleration.history: Enrico Fermi in 1949 first mathematically described the process of accelerating cosmic rays, assuming interaction with magnetic clouds. Later, in the 1970s, Anatoly Vlasov, Vitaly Ginzburg, Sergey Syrovatsky, and independently Roger Blandford and Ralph Ostriker developed the theory of shock acceleration, which became the standard model for generating nonthermal radiation in astrophysical objects.
fermi_acceleration.how_it_works: It is applied to explain the radio spectra of supernova remnants, active galactic nuclei, solar flares. However, the mechanism has an energy ceiling because the accelerated particle may eventually leave the acceleration region due to diffusion or energy losses from synchrotron radiation.
key_problems: The source of the highest-energy cosmic rays (above 10^18 eV) is not fully clear; classical Fermi acceleration requires extremely large sizes and magnetic fields.
The details of particle interaction with turbulence are complex and require numerical modeling.
Acceleration of electrons is complicated by their rapid radiation losses.

formulas.description: Average relative energy gain per cycle in shock front acceleration
formulas.meaning: 〈ΔE〉 is the average increase in particle energy per cycle of shock front crossings (J); E is the current energy of the particle (J); V_s is the speed of the shock wave in the observer's frame (m/s); c is the speed of light in vacuum (299,792,458 m/s)
N(E) is the differential concentration of particles with energy E (particles per unit energy); p is the spectral index, for a strong shock p ≈ 2

sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.name: Sunyaev–Zeldovich effect
sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.mini: Hot gas in a galaxy cluster heats up the echo of the Big Bang: photons of the CMB, colliding with plasma electrons, steal bits of energy from them.
sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.practical_application: An independent method to measure distances to galaxy clusters and study the large-scale structure of the Universe.
sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.description_popular: In 1969, Soviet astrophysicists Rashid Sunyaev and Yakov Zeldovich predicted that hot intergalactic gas in galaxy clusters should leave an imprint on the cosmic microwave background. Plasma electrons, accelerated to enormous speeds, transfer energy to photons via inverse Compton scattering. The effect is like a 'fog' of electrons that slightly tints the background light. This allows us to see galaxy clusters at any distance, since the surface brightness of the effect does not depend on distance.
sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.fun_fact_popular: The Sunyaev-Zeldovich effect was one of the first methods to confirm the existence of dark energy, when it was used to measure distances to distant clusters and compare them with the expansion model.
sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.description_simple: The cosmic microwave background is a faint microwave light left over from the Big Bang, uniformly filling the cosmos. Passing through huge clouds of hot gas in galaxy clusters, some photons collide with fast electrons. Electrons, having high energy (temperatures of millions of degrees), transfer some of this energy to photons, increasing their frequency. As a result, in the direction of the cluster, the spectrum of the CMB is slightly distorted—it becomes slightly 'hotter' at certain wavelengths.
sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.how_it_works_simple: Astronomers observe this distortion and use it to determine the gas pressure in the cluster, its mass, and its distance, independent of redshift.
sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.fun_fact: In the Bullet Cluster, overlaying maps of the SZ effect and gravitational lensing directly separated the distributions of ordinary and dark matter during a cluster collision, which became a classic proof of dark matter's existence.
sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.description: The thermal Sunyaev–Zeldovich effect is a spectral distortion of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) caused by inverse Compton scattering of CMB photons by hot electrons (T_e ~ 10⁷–10⁸ K) within galaxy clusters. This scattering gives low-energy CMB photons additional energy, leading to a characteristic change in brightness temperature: below ~218 GHz there is a deficit, above—an excess. The magnitude of the effect is described by the Compton y-parameter y = (σ_T/(m_e c^2)) ∫ n_e k_B T_e dl, where σ_T is the Thomson scattering cross-section. The kinetic SZ effect additionally shifts the frequency due to the Doppler shift.
sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.history: Predicted in 1969 by Rashid Sunyaev and Yakov Zeldovich in the context of studying the interaction of the CMB with hot intergalactic plasma. Nearly two decades later, in the early 1980s, the effect was first detected in clusters (e.g., the Coma cluster) using radio telescopes. It was further developed with the launch of space observatories WMAP and Planck, which provided detailed SZ effect maps, and with ground-based instruments like SPT and ACT. Sunyaev and Zeldovich also described the kinetic version of the effect, related to the bulk motion of the cluster.
sunyaev_zeldovich_effect.how_it_works: Used in cosmology to detect and study galaxy clusters at high redshifts, estimate their mass and structure. Because the surface brightness of the SZ effect does not depend on distance (unlike apparent brightness), it serves as a powerful tool for cosmological tests, including constraints on dark energy parameters and cosmological models. The applicability is limited by the need to accurately separate the SZ signal from primary CMB anisotropy, as well as from dust and synchrotron emission.
key_problems: Extracting the weak SZ signal against the primary CMB fluctuations requires highly sensitive multi-frequency observations.
Signal contamination by dust emission from the clusters themselves and foreground/background galaxies.
The need to model the gas pressure profile for accurate mass determination—non-Gaussianity of the distribution introduces uncertainty.

formulas.latex: y = \frac{\sigma_T}{m_e c^2} \int n_e k_B T_e \, dl
formulas.meaning: y — dimensionless Comptonization parameter, σ_T ≈ 6.652×10⁻²⁹ m² — Thomson scattering cross-section of an electron, m_e ≈ 9.109×10⁻³¹ kg — electron mass, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s — speed of light, n_e — electron concentration (m⁻³), k_B ≈ 1.381×10⁻²³ J/K — Boltzmann constant, T_e — electron temperature (K), dl — line-of-sight length element (m).
ΔT_CMB — change in CMB temperature (K), T_CMB ≈ 2.725 K — average CMB temperature, y — Compton parameter, x = hν/(k_B T_CMB) — dimensionless frequency, h ≈ 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s — Planck constant.

sachs_wolfe_effect.name: Sachs–Wolfe effect
sachs_wolfe_effect.mini: The universal lullaby is recorded with spots because light climbed out of gravitational wells and lost energy—like a tired traveler climbing a hill.
sachs_wolfe_effect.practical_application: CMB maps use the Sachs–Wolfe effect to look back to the recombination epoch and test inflation models.
sachs_wolfe_effect.description_popular: In 1967, Rainer K. Sachs and Arthur M. Wolfe showed that gravitational redshift explains the main anisotropies of the CMB. Before that, cosmologists puzzled over the origin of the microwave sky's patchiness. The idea is simple: a photon leaving a gravitational well loses energy, while one falling into a well gains it. But at the time of CMB emission, the wells were shallow, and photons escaped almost freely, slightly cooled. The total temperature change is proportional to the difference in gravitational potential at the emission point and on Earth. Since Earth is in a very weak field, the main contribution comes from the potential at the last scattering surface. It's like launching balls from different depths of a pool: the deeper, the slower the ball emerges to the surface.
sachs_wolfe_effect.fun_fact_popular: Thanks to the Sachs–Wolfe effect, astronomers can 'weigh' the most distant galaxy clusters without even seeing them: from the temperature of spots, they estimate the gravitational potential at recombination.
sachs_wolfe_effect.description_simple: The Sachs–Wolfe effect explains why images of the CMB (the ancient 'echo' of the Big Bang) have spots with slightly different temperatures. Light emitted 13.8 billion years ago, when the Universe became transparent (recombination epoch), traveled to us through a changing gravitational field. If a photon (particle of light) started from a region with stronger gravity (where spacetime is 'curved'), it spent energy escaping outward, and its frequency dropped—this is gravitational redshift. Such photons reach us slightly 'colder'. Conversely, photons from less dense regions lose less energy and appear 'hotter'. Thus gravity directly paints the temperature map of the early Universe.
sachs_wolfe_effect.how_it_works_simple: The effect contributes the main difference in temperature between points on the CMB map for angles larger than a couple of degrees. It is used to reconstruct the distribution of dark matter at the time of recombination.
sachs_wolfe_effect.fun_fact: If radiation had dominated the Universe at recombination instead of matter, the sign of the effect would be reversed—denser regions would appear hotter.
sachs_wolfe_effect.description: The Sachs–Wolfe effect is a change in CMB temperature caused by gravitational redshift as photons climb out of overdense regions on the last scattering surface. In linear perturbation theory, the relative temperature change is ΔT/T = -Φ/(3c²), where Φ is the Newtonian gravitational potential at recombination. Additionally, there is the integrated Sachs–Wolfe effect, arising if the potential changes along the photon path (e.g., due to dark energy). The effect gives the main contribution to anisotropy on large angular scales (l < 100) and, together with acoustic peaks, is used to determine cosmological parameters.
sachs_wolfe_effect.history: In 1967, Rainer Sachs and Arthur Wolfe first described the mechanism of temperature fluctuations in the microwave background due to gravitational redshift. Their paper 'Perturbations of a Cosmological Model and Angular Variations of the Microwave Background' laid the foundation of modern CMB physics. Later, in the 1980s, with the development of inflationary cosmology, the effect became key to predicting the primordial perturbation spectrum. Measurements by satellites COBE, WMAP, and Planck confirmed the contribution of the Sachs–Wolfe effect with high precision, including the integrated version linked to dark energy.
sachs_wolfe_effect.how_it_works: The effect is used to extract information about dark matter distribution at recombination and constrain dark energy models via the integrated Sachs–Wolfe effect. It works within linear perturbation theory in GR under the condition of small fluctuation amplitudes. On small angular scales, other effects dominate (acoustic oscillations, Silk damping). The integrated effect is noticeable only in the presence of dynamical dark energy, making it a sensitive probe of cosmic expansion.
key_problems: Need to separate the primary effect from secondary contributions (lensing, Sunyaev–Zeldovich)
Degeneracy between the primordial spectrum amplitude and the optical depth to recombination
The integrated effect is weak and requires removing the contribution from large-scale structure

formulas.description: Relative change in CMB temperature due to the Sachs–Wolfe effect
formulas.meaning: ΔT/T — relative CMB temperature fluctuation; Φ — gravitational potential (positive for regions of compression), measured in m²/s²; c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s — speed of light

jeans_instability.name: Jeans instability
jeans_instability.mini: A gas cloud collapses into a star when gravity overcomes internal pressure—like too thick a layer of snow buckling a roof.
jeans_instability.practical_application: Jeans instability determines the threshold for interstellar cloud collapse into protostars and is used in numerical models of star formation.
jeans_instability.description_popular: In 1902, British physicist James Jeans examined the condition for gravitational collapse of a self-gravitating gas sphere. He realized that collapse begins when the free-fall time becomes shorter than the sound-crossing time. If a density perturbation cannot be smoothed out by sound, gravity locally amplifies it—an avalanche-like contraction ensues. From wave analysis in a homogeneous medium, a critical length λ_J ~ c_s / √(G ρ) is derived. Regions larger than λ_J are unstable. Thus nature automatically carves matter into stellar 'loaves'.
jeans_instability.fun_fact_popular: The Sun itself once formed from a cloud fragment that lost Jeans stability about 4.6 billion years ago.
jeans_instability.description_simple: Jeans instability is the physical mechanism that triggers star birth. Imagine a huge cold cloud of hydrogen in space. Thermal motion of particles (pressure) tries to expand it, while gravity tries to contract it. If the cloud is sufficiently massive and cold, gravity wins, and the cloud begins to collapse, fragmenting into clumps. These clumps become protostars. A key concept is the Jeans length: there is a critical size beyond which any density enhancement will inexorably contract. If the cloud's size exceeds this length, it is doomed to collapse.
jeans_instability.how_it_works_simple: In stellar nurseries like the Orion Nebula, giant molecular clouds exceed the Jeans length and fragment into hundreds of new suns. Without this instability, galaxies would have remained thin gas.
jeans_instability.fun_fact: The classical Jeans mass for a typical molecular cloud is about 1 M☉, which surprisingly matches the characteristic mass of observed stars.
jeans_instability.description: Jeans instability is a criterion for gravitational collapse in a homogeneous isotropic medium. For a gas with density ρ and sound speed c_s, perturbations with wavelength λ > λ_J = √(π c_s² / (G ρ)) grow exponentially with time. Alternatively, a spherical cloud mass exceeding the Jeans mass M_J = (π/6) c_s³ / (G^(3/2) ρ^(1/2)) will contract. The instability is suppressed by magnetic fields, rotation, and turbulence, but remains the cornerstone of star formation theories and the formation of large-scale structure in the Universe.
jeans_instability.history: James Hopwood Jeans published his analysis of gravitational instability in 1902 within Newtonian gravity. He mathematically showed how small density perturbations in an infinite medium can grow if the wavelength exceeds a critical value. Later, the theory was generalized to account for the expansion of the Universe (E.M. Lifshitz in 1946) and relativistic effects. Modern observational data on star clusters and infrared images of star-forming regions brilliantly confirm the Jeans fragmentation scenario.
jeans_instability.how_it_works: In astrophysics, Jeans instability is used to estimate collapse conditions for molecular clouds and the formation of planetary systems in protoplanetary disks. It is applicable to gas regions with weak magnetic fields and negligible rotation; under rapid rotation or strong magnetization, the critical mass increases. In cosmology, the Jeans length determines the scale at which primordial density perturbations begin to grow.
key_problems: Considering turbulence and magnetic fields significantly complicates the simple Jeans model
The need to explain fragmentation efficiency in real clouds with subcritical mass
Feedback from already formed stars (radiation, outflows) is not included in the classical theory

formulas.meaning: λ_J — Jeans length, m; c_s — sound speed in the medium, m/s; G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²) — gravitational constant; ρ — gas density, kg/m³
M_J — Jeans mass, kg; c_s — sound speed, m/s; G — gravitational constant; ρ — gas density, kg/m³

lense_thirring_effect.name: Lense–Thirring effect
lense_thirring_effect.mini: A massive rotating sphere drags the very vacuum around itself—sticky like honey pulling a spoon. A nearby satellite feels the whirlpool.
lense_thirring_effect.practical_application: The Lense–Thirring effect is accounted for in GPS navigation satellites and measured using high-precision gyroscopes like Gravity Probe B to test General Relativity.
lense_thirring_effect.description_popular: Austrian physicists Josef Lense and Hans Thirring in 1918, just three years after the creation of GR, found an exact solution for the weak field of a slowly rotating sphere. They showed that the spacetime metric acquires additional components that cause test bodies to precess. The gravitational field of a rotating object splits into a static part (like that of a Newtonian sphere) and a 'gravitomagnetic' part, producing forces similar to magnetic ones. Thus the concept of gravitomagnetism was born.
lense_thirring_effect.fun_fact_popular: The Lense–Thirring effect is so tiny that the gyroscopes of Gravity Probe B had to be the most spherical objects ever made by humans, with deviations from a perfect sphere of only 40 atomic layers.
lense_thirring_effect.description_simple: The Lense–Thirring effect, or frame-dragging, is a prediction of Einstein's General Relativity. A massive body, as it rotates, drags spacetime around itself, much like an electric motor creates a magnetic field. If another object passes nearby or a gyroscope spins, its axis slowly turns in the direction of the central body's rotation. This is a very weak effect: even for Earth, it shifts the orbit of a satellite by only a few meters per year.
lense_thirring_effect.how_it_works_simple: To notice the effect, bodies with enormous angular momentum are needed—neutron stars or black holes. For Earth, its existence was confirmed only in 2011 using the LAGEOS and LARES satellites, tracking tiny orbital shifts.
lense_thirring_effect.fun_fact: For a black hole with maximal spin, the frame-dragging at the event horizon is so strong that no particle can stay on a stationary orbit without a specific direction of rotation.
lense_thirring_effect.description: The Lense–Thirring effect is the relativistic dragging of inertial frames by a rotating gravitating body, manifesting as precession of the angular momentum vector of a test particle (gyroscope) around the direction of the central object's rotation. In the weak field limit, the angular precession frequency Ω = 2GJ/(c² r³), where J is the angular momentum of the body, r is the distance. For arbitrary orbital orientation, the vector Ω is described by a more complex expression. The effect also leads to precession of satellite orbital nodes (Lense–Thirring precession) and dragging of the orbital plane.
lense_thirring_effect.history: Josef Lense and Hans Thirring in 1918 first derived the formula for gyroscope precession in a rotating gravitational field from Einstein's equations. Their work 'Über den Einfluß der Eigenrotation der Zentralkörper auf die Bewegung der Planeten und Monde nach der Einsteinschen Gravitationstheorie' laid the foundations of gravitomagnetism. For a long time, the effect remained unconfirmed due to its extreme smallness for planets. Only in 2004–2011 did the satellite missions Gravity Probe B and LAGEOS/LARES confirm the GR prediction with an accuracy of about 10%.
lense_thirring_effect.how_it_works: The effect is taken into account in high-precision satellite positioning (e.g., orbit corrections in GPS/GLONASS systems). For compact objects like neutron stars and black holes, frame-dragging can cause observable precession of accretion disks and jets. In strong fields, the weak-field approximation is inapplicable, and numerical solutions of the full Einstein equations are required.
key_problems: Difficulty in separating the gravitomagnetic effect from Newtonian perturbations (Earth's non-uniform gravitational field)
Requires multi-year data collection for a statistically significant signal
Higher-order effects for rapidly rotating black holes are still actively researched

formulas.description: Angular precession frequency of the Lense–Thirring effect for a test particle in the equatorial plane
formulas.meaning: Ω — angular precession frequency, rad/s; G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²) — gravitational constant; J — angular momentum of the central body, kg·m²/s; c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s — speed of light; r — distance from the center of the body to the test particle, m

saha_equation.name: Saha equation
saha_equation.mini: Stellar chemical analysis: atoms lose electrons when heated, like ice melting in tea. The Saha equation predicts how many atoms are ionized at a given temperature.
saha_equation.practical_application: Astrophysicists determine the temperature and chemical composition of stars from absorption lines in spectra, using ionization balance.
saha_equation.description_popular: In 1920, Indian physicist Meghnad Saha combined thermodynamics and quantum mechanics to explain the solar spectrum. The essence is simple: in collisions within a hot gas, atoms can become ionized, i.e., lose electrons. The equation relates the concentrations of neutral atoms, ions, and electrons to temperature and pressure. It's like a balance between lotteries and prizes: the more expensive the prize (ionization energy), the fewer 'customers' (ions). The equation became the key to deciphering starlight.
saha_equation.fun_fact_popular: Using the Saha equation, it was first correctly determined that the Sun is mostly hydrogen, not iron, as previously thought.
saha_equation.description_simple: The air in a rarefied neon light glows because electricity ionizes the gas—stripping electrons from atoms. The Saha equation describes how many ions (atoms missing one or more electrons) and free electrons arise when a gas is heated to a certain temperature. The hotter it is, the stronger the ionization. It is like a balance where on one pan is the energy of thermal motion of particles (k_B T, where k_B is Boltzmann's constant, T is temperature), and on the other is the energy needed to rip an electron from an atom (ionization energy).
saha_equation.how_it_works_simple: In stellar spectra, dark lines are seen—some colors are absorbed by atoms. From the depth of these lines, using the Saha equation, one can learn how many atoms are in which ionization state, and thus the temperature and density of the gas.
saha_equation.fun_fact: The Saha equation predicted that in the atmospheres of cool stars, hydrogen is nearly neutral, while in hot stars it is almost completely ionized, explaining the sharp change in spectral classes.
saha_equation.description: The Saha equation (ionization equilibrium) is a fundamental result of statistical physics describing the degree of thermal ionization of a gas. It is based on the equality of chemical potentials for ionization reactions under conditions of local thermodynamic equilibrium. For the reaction A_i ↔ A_{i+1} + e^-, where A_i is an atom in ionization state i, the equilibrium condition leads to a dependence of concentrations on temperature T, electron density n_e, and partition functions Z. It is especially important in astrophysics for interpreting stellar spectra.
saha_equation.history: Meghnad Saha, an Indian physicist and astrophysicist, derived the equation in 1920 while working on the theory of stellar atmospheres. He developed the ideas of Arthur Stanley Eddington and Karl Schwarzschild. Later, Irving Langmuir generalized the equation to multi-electron ionization, and Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin used it to analyze the solar composition, proving the dominance of hydrogen.
saha_equation.how_it_works: It is applied in stellar spectroscopy: from the observed distribution of absorption lines of different ions, the electron temperature and density in stellar atmospheres are determined. Limitations: it assumes local thermodynamic equilibrium, which breaks down in rarefied nebulae or in the presence of strong non-thermal radiation sources.
key_problems: Not applicable for non-equilibrium conditions, such as in chromospheres or supernova remnants
Dependence on partition functions requires accounting for many excited levels
Difficulty in accurately accounting for Coulomb interaction in dense plasma
