Interestingly, Hooke accused Huygens of plagiarizing the clock mechanism, but ultimately the law became his main contribution. Later, Thomas Young and Cauchy generalized the theory of elasticity to continuous media, introducing the concepts of stress and strain.
hookes_law.how_it_works: Hooke's law is the foundation of elasticity theory, used in construction, mechanical engineering, and seismology. It determines the behavior of solids under small loads, allowing calculation of deformations in bridges, skyscrapers, and machine components. Exceeding the yield point leads to plasticity or fracture. At the nanoscale, the discreteness of the atomic structure and quantum effects can modify the linear law.
key_problems: Not all materials are linear even in the elastic region (rubber, biological tissues are described by nonlinear models).
Viscoelasticity: under prolonged loading, many polymers and glasses 'flow', obeying not pure elasticity.
At high frequencies (e.g., in ultrasonic vibration), internal friction and inertia break the simple linear relationship.

formulas.meaning: F — elastic force (vector, N), exerted by the deformed body; k — stiffness coefficient (positive constant, N/m), characterizing the elastic properties of a specific spring; x — displacement vector (from equilibrium, m). The minus sign shows that the force is directed opposite to the displacement.

formulas.description: Generalized Hooke's law for an isotropic body under uniaxial tension/compression
formulas.meaning: σ — mechanical stress (Pa), force per unit cross-sectional area; E — Young's modulus (modulus of elasticity, Pa), a fundamental material characteristic (for steel E ≈ 2.1×10¹¹ Pa, for rubber E ~ 10⁶ Pa); ε — strain (dimensionless), ε = ΔL/L₀, where ΔL is the change in length, L₀ is the original length.

archimedes_principle.name: Archimedes' principle
archimedes_principle.mini: A body submerged in a fluid loses exactly as much weight as the weight of the fluid it displaces. The bathtub overflows when you get in — it's not just an inconvenience, but a demonstration of the law.
archimedes_principle.practical_application: Archimedes' principle explains the buoyancy of ships, the operation of hydrometers, and the behavior of submarines during diving and surfacing.
archimedes_principle.description_popular: Archimedes of Syracuse, according to legend, discovered his principle in the bath, shouting 'Eureka!'. The principle itself: a body submerged in a fluid or gas experiences a buoyant force equal to the weight of the displaced medium. This force arises from the pressure difference: the pressure is greater at the bottom than at the top, and this difference creates a lifting force. Buoyancy is determined by the density ratio: if the density of the body is less than that of the fluid, it floats.
archimedes_principle.fun_fact_popular: The Dead Sea is so salty (density 1.24 kg/L versus 1.00 for fresh water) that a person effortlessly lies on the surface without sinking.
archimedes_principle.description_simple: When you place an object in a fluid (water, oil, mercury), a buoyant force acts on it. It is directed upward and equals the weight of the fluid the object displaced. Density (how heavy a substance is for its size) determines whether the object sinks or floats. The denser the fluid, the greater the weight it can support.
archimedes_principle.how_it_works_simple: Ships made of steel don't sink because their volume is large and they displace a lot of water. Fish control buoyancy with a swim bladder. Balloons in the atmosphere obey a similar law, only for gases.
archimedes_principle.fun_fact: According to legend, Archimedes not only shouted but ran naked through the streets of Syracuse. Modern research shows that to detect the crown substitution, he would have needed volume measurement accuracy on the order of 0.1 ml — a difficult task without precise vessels.
archimedes_principle.description: Any body submerged in a fluid (gas) at rest experiences a buoyant force from this fluid (gas) equal to the weight of the fluid (gas) displaced by the body. The force is directed vertically upward and is applied at the center of gravity of the displaced volume (center of buoyancy).
archimedes_principle.history: Archimedes (287–212 BC) formulated the principle in his treatise 'On Floating Bodies'. Legend says he discovered it while solving the problem of the authenticity of Hiero II's golden crown. Archimedes realized that the volume of displaced water allows measuring the volume of an irregularly shaped body and calculating its density.
archimedes_principle.how_it_works: Applied in shipbuilding, design of underwater vehicles, balloons, densitometers and flow meters. Limits of applicability: the law is valid for statically resting fluids and gases. In non-inertial systems or with accelerated fluid motion, corrections are needed.
key_problems: The law does not account for viscosity and surface tension, which can affect the submersion of small bodies.
In inhomogeneous fluids (e.g., with a salinity gradient), calculations become more complex.
For bodies with high entry speed into water, hydrodynamic effects must be considered.

formulas.meaning: F_buoy — buoyant force (N), ρ — density of the fluid or gas (kg/m³), g — acceleration of gravity (approximately 9.8 m/s²), V — volume of displaced fluid (m³)

zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.name: zeroth law of thermodynamics
zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.mini: If a cup of hot tea touches a table, and the table touches a handle, the handle's temperature will eventually equal the tea's temperature. Thermal equilibrium is contagious.
zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.practical_application: A thermometer works precisely because it reaches equilibrium with the object being measured, and we read the common temperature.
zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.description_popular: Thermodynamics begins with the zeroth law because it was recognized later than the first and second, but turned out to be more fundamental. It was formulated by Ralph Fowler in 1931. The essence: thermal equilibrium is transitive. If coffee in a thermos and the air in the room have the same temperature, and the air in the room has the same as a book on the shelf, then the coffee and the book also 'agree' on temperature.
zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.fun_fact_popular: Thermometers appeared long before formal thermodynamics. Galileo created a thermoscope as early as 1593, not knowing that it embodied the zeroth law.
zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.description_simple: The zeroth law of thermodynamics states: if two objects each have the same temperature as a third, then they have the same temperature as each other. Temperature is a measure of how 'hot' a body is, and it equalizes upon contact. This principle allows us to measure temperature with a thermometer: we wait until the thermometer heats or cools to the temperature of the patient or room.
zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.how_it_works_simple: All thermometers — mercury, electronic, infrared — rely on the zeroth law. Even when you touch a forehead and decide if there's a fever, your hand compares heat with the body.
zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.fun_fact: The name 'zeroth law' arose almost as a joke: the first three laws were already numbered 1-3, and the fundamental principle was left unnumbered. They had to introduce a zeroth one to avoid shifting the established numbering.
zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.description: The zeroth law of thermodynamics postulates: if two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other. This transitivity of equilibrium serves as the basis for introducing temperature as a state function and for the correctness of all temperature measurements.
zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.history: Although the idea of temperature is as ancient as observations of cooling, the formalization of the law occurred in the 20th century. Ralph Fowler in 1931, while discussing the principles of statistical mechanics, called this postulate 'zeroth' because it logically precedes the first and second laws, formulated earlier. The term quickly caught on.
zeroth_law_of_thermodynamics.how_it_works: The law underlies all thermometry and calorimetry. Without it, the concept of temperature would be undefined. Limits of applicability: the law holds for macroscopic systems in equilibrium. In non-equilibrium processes, for nanoscale objects, or in strong gravitational fields, refinements may be needed.
key_problems: The law assumes that thermal equilibrium is a state where macroscopic parameters do not change. However, quantum fluctuations in the microworld call into question the existence of strict equilibrium for small systems.
The definition of temperature is not entirely unambiguous in the presence of long-range forces or in curved spacetime.

formulas.latex: T_A = T_B \land T_B = T_C \implies T_A = T_C
formulas.meaning: T_A, T_B, T_C — temperatures of systems A, B, C; the condition T_A = T_B and T_B = T_C implies T_A = T_C

third_law_of_thermodynamics.name: third law of thermodynamics
third_law_of_thermodynamics.mini: Absolute zero is unattainable, like the horizon. As you approach it, everything freezes, and even disorder (entropy) tends to zero.
third_law_of_thermodynamics.practical_application: This limitation prevents perpetual motion machines from working, explains why something cannot be cooled exactly to −273.15°C, and sets the efficiency limit for refrigerators.
third_law_of_thermodynamics.description_popular: Walther Nernst in 1906 suggested that at absolute zero, the entropy of chemical reactions becomes zero. Later, Max Planck generalized this to the statement that the entropy of any perfect crystal at T=0 is zero. Entropy is a measure of disorder or the number of ways a system can be arranged at the micro-level; at absolute zero, only one, the most ordered, is possible.
third_law_of_thermodynamics.fun_fact_popular: A kettle will never boil by itself, absorbing heat from the room — the third law prevents it, narrowing the path to free energy.
third_law_of_thermodynamics.description_simple: The third law of thermodynamics states that it is impossible to cool a substance to absolute zero (−273.15°C). As you approach this cold, entropy (a measure of disorder) decreases, and in an ideal crystal at zero it becomes zero. This means the molecules freeze in a perfectly ordered structure.
third_law_of_thermodynamics.how_it_works_simple: Scientists use laser cooling and adiabatic demagnetization to reach temperatures billionths of a degree above absolute zero, but never zero itself. Even in space, where it is very cold, there always remains about 2.7 degrees of warmth from the Big Bang.
third_law_of_thermodynamics.fun_fact: The record temperature achieved in the lab is 100 picokelvins (10⁻¹⁰ K) in a Bose-Einstein condensate, which is tens of billions of times colder than interstellar space, yet still not absolute zero.
third_law_of_thermodynamics.description: The third law of thermodynamics: as temperature tends to absolute zero, the entropy of an equilibrium system approaches a constant value, independent of pressure, state of aggregation, and other parameters. In Planck's formulation for an ideal crystal, this value is zero. The law forbids reaching absolute zero in a finite number of operations.
third_law_of_thermodynamics.history: Walther Nernst, studying chemical reactions at low temperatures, formulated the heat theorem in 1906: the entropy change in a reversible isothermal reaction tends to zero as absolute zero is approached. Max Planck in 1911 gave it a modern statistical interpretation, linking entropy to the number of microstates and postulating that at T=0 this number equals one.
third_law_of_thermodynamics.how_it_works: The law defines the reference point for entropy and allows calculation of absolute entropy values for substances. Applied in cryophysics, in achieving ultra-low temperatures, in superconducting magnet technology. Limits: the law holds for equilibrium systems; glasses and amorphous bodies may have residual entropy at T=0 due to frozen-in disorder.
key_problems: Glasses and disordered systems retain non-zero entropy at arbitrarily low temperatures, violating Planck's formulation.
From the point of view of quantum mechanics, the ground state of a system may be degenerate, leading to non-zero entropy.
Achieving temperatures on the order of nanokelvins and below still involves enormous technical difficulties, but there are no new fundamental limits — only practical ones.

formulas.meaning: S — entropy of the system, T — absolute temperature (K); the limit of entropy as temperature tends to zero is zero for an ideal crystal
S — entropy, k_B — Boltzmann constant (≈ 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K), Ω — number of microstates; at T = 0 for a perfect crystal Ω = 1, therefore S = 0

ideal_gas_law.name: ideal gas law
ideal_gas_law.mini: Gas molecules are like restless schoolchildren in a gym: the more they are cornered (volume reduced) or made to run faster (heated), the more often they hit the walls — that's how pressure rises.
ideal_gas_law.practical_application: From inflating tires to weather forecasting: the relationship between pressure, volume, and temperature governs engines, balloons, and breathing.
ideal_gas_law.description_popular: In 1834, Émile Clapeyron combined the experimental laws of Boyle, Charles, and Gay-Lussac into the single equation pV = nRT. This elegant formula describes the equilibrium state of an ideal gas — a model where molecules are considered point-like and non-interacting. It works as a 'switch' between the volume, pressure, and temperature of a gas, allowing prediction of the behavior of real gases under ordinary conditions.
ideal_gas_law.fun_fact_popular: If you leave a ball in the sun, it inflates itself — not magic, but the temperature increase raises the pressure with almost constant volume.
ideal_gas_law.description_simple: Gases consist of tiny particles (molecules) that rush around chaotically. Gas pressure is the sum of their impacts on the walls. The equation links four quantities: pressure (force per area), volume (the space the gas occupies), temperature (a measure of the molecules' energy), and amount of substance (number of molecules). If you heat a gas at constant volume, the pressure rises because the molecules hit more often and harder.
ideal_gas_law.how_it_works_simple: A balloon slightly expands in the sun — a demonstration of the law. In engines, a piston moves under the pressure of hot gases. Meteorologists calculate the expansion of air when heated. Breathing: the lungs change volume, and air enters due to the pressure difference.
ideal_gas_law.fun_fact: The concept of the universal gas constant R came later, when Dmitri Mendeleev in 1874 derived the equation for one mole of gas, linking it to Avogadro's number.
ideal_gas_law.description: An ideal gas is a theoretical model where particles have no volume of their own and do not interact at a distance, undergoing only elastic collisions. The equation of state pV = nRT establishes the relationship between macroscopic parameters. It is derived from the kinetic theory of gases, where pressure is expressed through the average kinetic energy of molecules: p = (2/3) n ⟨E_k⟩. The internal energy of an ideal gas depends only on temperature: for a monatomic gas U = (3/2) nRT. The law is the limiting case for real gases at low pressures and high temperatures.
ideal_gas_law.history: The experiments of Robert Boyle (1662) and Edme Mariotte showed the inverse proportionality of pressure and volume (pV=const at constant T). Jacques Charles (1787) and Joseph Gay-Lussac (1802) established the dependence of volume on temperature. Amedeo Avogadro in 1811 hypothesized that equal volumes of gases contain equal numbers of molecules under the same conditions. Émile Clapeyron in 1834 combined these laws into a single equation, later generalized by Dmitri Mendeleev with the inclusion of the universal gas constant.
ideal_gas_law.how_it_works: Widely applied from internal combustion engine calculations to atmospheric modeling. Limits of applicability: the equation is not applicable at high densities and low temperatures, when intermolecular interactions and the finite size of molecules become significant — there, Van der Waals equations with corrections are used.
key_problems: Does not account for quantum effects and degeneracy at ultra-low temperatures
Does not describe phase transitions
For polyatomic gases, internal energy is more complex due to rotational and vibrational degrees of freedom

formulas.meaning: p — gas pressure (Pa), V — volume (m³), n — amount of substance (mol), R — universal gas constant (R ≈ 8.314 J/(mol·K)), T — absolute temperature (K)

formulas.description: Internal energy of a monatomic ideal gas
formulas.meaning: U — internal energy (J), n — amount of substance (mol), R — universal gas constant (R ≈ 8.314 J/(mol·K)), T — absolute temperature (K)
